- **Substring:** $w = p \cdot u \cdot q$ for some $p, q$. Must be **contiguous** (not "subsequence").
- **Monoid Structure:** $\langle \Sigma^*, \cdot, \varepsilon \rangle$ forms a monoid — closure + associativity + identity.
- **Always show both base case and inductive step** in induction proofs over $k$.
- **Always state both sides of an identity** in algebraic verification problems (compute directly, then apply the rule, then compare).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Languages, Regular Languages - Deterministic Finite State Automata (DFA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT419 - INTRODUCTION TO THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Languages, Regular Languages - Deterministic Finite State Automata (DFA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Alphabet, Strings, and Languages (Foundation Concepts)

Before defining the Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA), we must first understand the foundational objects it operates on. The hierarchy of formal language theory begins with three primitive objects: **symbols**, **strings**, and **languages**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alphabet (Σ):** A finite, non-empty set of symbols. For example, $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ is the binary alphabet, while $\Sigma = \{a, b, c, \ldots, z\}$ is a letter alphabet. The KTU 2024 scheme defines alphabets as **finite** sets without any ordering requirement.

> [!NOTE]
> **String (or Word):** A finite sequence of symbols drawn from a given alphabet $\Sigma$. The **length of a string** $w$, denoted $\vert w \vert$, is the number of symbols it contains. The unique string of length zero is called the **empty string**, denoted by $\varepsilon$ (epsilon) or $\lambda$ (lambda). The set of all strings over $\Sigma$ (including $\varepsilon$) is denoted $\Sigma^*$, and the set of all non-empty strings is $\Sigma^+$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Language:** A **language** $L$ over an alphabet $\Sigma$ is any **subset** of $\Sigma^*$. That is, $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$. Since languages are sets, the standard set operations (union, intersection, complement, concatenation, Kleene star) apply to them. Examples include:
> - $L_1 = \{w \in \{0,1\}^* \mid w \text{ starts with } 0\}$
> - $L_2 = \emptyset$ (the empty language — contains no strings)
> - $L_3 = \{\varepsilon\}$ (language containing only the empty string)

### Intuitive Analogy: The Alphabet–String–Language Triangle

Think of an **alphabet** as the **scattered LEGO bricks** on a table (the symbols $a, b, c, \ldots$). A **string** is a **specific LEGO tower** built by stacking bricks in a particular order (e.g., $a b b a c$). A **language** is the **entire collection box** of LEGO towers you have built — it could contain infinitely many towers, just one tower, or even be empty. The DFA, then, is the **robot quality inspector** that opens this box, picks up each tower one brick at a time, and decides *yes* (accept) or *no* (reject).

---

## 1.2 Regular Languages (The Target Class)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regular Language (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A language $L$ is called a **regular language** if there exists a **Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)** $M$ such that $L = L(M)$, where $L(M)$ is the language **accepted/recognized** by $M$. Equivalently, a language is regular if it can be described by a **regular expression**.

The set of all regular languages over an alphabet $\Sigma$ is denoted $\text{REG}(\Sigma)$ or simply $\mathcal{R}$. This class forms the **innermost (smallest) layer** of the **Chomsky Hierarchy**:

$$
\text{Regular} \subset \text{Context-Free} \subset \text{Context-Sensitive} \subset \text{Recursively Enumerable}
$$

### Intuitive Analogy: The Vending Machine

A **vending machine** is the perfect real-world embodiment of a regular-language recognizer. It accepts only a specific, pre-programmed set of coin sequences (e.g., ₹5, ₹10, ₹15, ₹20, but not ₹7 or ₹13). The set of *valid* coin sequences forms a **regular language**, and the machine's internal logic is precisely a **finite automaton** — it has a finite number of states (the running total), deterministic transitions (each coin type leads to exactly one next state), and accepts when the total equals the price.

---

## 1.3 Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA) — The Central Object

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA):** A DFA is a 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where:
> - $Q$ is a **finite** set of **states**.
> - $\Sigma$ is a **finite** set of **input symbols** (the alphabet).
> - $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ is the **transition function**.
> - $q_0 \in Q$ is the **start state** (initial state).
> - $F \subseteq Q$ is the set of **accepting (final) states**.

The keyword **deterministic** means that for every state $q \in Q$ and every symbol $a \in \Sigma$, there is **exactly one** transition defined — i.e., $\delta(q, a)$ returns a **single, unique** state. This is in stark contrast to the Nondeterministic Finite Automaton (NFA), which we will study later.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Elevator Controller

Imagine a **simple elevator** in a 2-floor building:
- **States** $Q = \{\text{Ground}, \text{First Floor}\}$
- **Input symbols** $\Sigma = \{\text{Up-Button, Down-Button}\}$
- **Start state** $q_0 = \text{Ground}$
- **Accepting state** $F = \{\text{First Floor}\}$

Each button press causes a **deterministic, predictable** state change. The "language" accepted by this elevator is the set of button-press sequences that leave the elevator on the **First Floor** (e.g., U, UUU, DUU, DUDUU — but **not** UD, because the final position is Ground, not First Floor). This is exactly the kind of membership-testing problem a DFA solves in formal language theory.

---

## 1.4 Why Study DFAs? (Engineering Motivation)

> [!NOTE]
> DFAs are not just theoretical curiosities. They are the **mathematical backbone** of:
> - **Lexical analyzers** in compilers (e.g., tokenizing `int`, `if`, identifiers, numbers).
> - **Regular-expression engines** in programming languages (used in `grep`, `sed`, `awk`, Python `re`, Java `Pattern`).
> - **Network protocol verification** (e.g., TCP state machines).
> - **Digital circuit design** (sequential logic with finite memory).
> - **Pattern matching in bioinformatics** (DNA motif search).
> - **Spell-checkers and input validators** in software applications.

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats the DFA as the **gateway concept** to all of formal language theory because it is the simplest, most visual, and most intuitive model of computation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the 5-Tuple: $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$

Let us dissect each component with the precision KTU examiners expect.

### Component 1: $Q$ — The Finite State Set
- $Q$ is a **finite, non-empty** set of internal "configurations" the machine can be in.
- The **cardinality** $\vert Q \vert$ is the **number of states**.
- Each state is a node in the state-transition diagram.
- The *finiteness* of $Q$ is the defining characteristic that places DFAs at the bottom of the computational hierarchy — they have **bounded memory**.

### Component 2: $\Sigma$ — The Input Alphabet
- $\Sigma$ is the **finite, non-empty** set of symbols the machine can read from the input tape.
- A DFA is **complete** with respect to $\Sigma$ iff for every $(q, a) \in Q \times \Sigma$, the transition $\delta(q, a)$ is defined. Some authors enforce totality; others allow partial DFAs (which are convertible).

### Component 3: $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ — The Transition Function
- This is the **program** of the DFA.
- Given a current state $q$ and the next input symbol $a$, $\delta(q, a)$ uniquely specifies the next state.
- **Determinism property:** $\forall q \in Q, \forall a \in \Sigma : \vert \delta(q, a) \vert = 1$.
- Visualized as a **directed edge labeled $a$** from state $q$ to state $\delta(q, a)$ in the state diagram.

### Component 4: $q_0 \in Q$ — The Start State
- The unique state in which computation begins.
- Marked in the state diagram with a **short incoming arrow** (no source state).
- If $w = \varepsilon$ (empty input), the machine halts immediately in $q_0$; hence $\varepsilon \in L(M)$ iff $q_0 \in F$.

### Component 5: $F \subseteq Q$ — The Set of Accepting (Final) States
- A subset of $Q$; could be empty (in which case $L(M) = \emptyset$).
- Marked in the state diagram with a **double circle**.
- A string is **accepted** iff the computation ends in a state belonging to $F$.

---

## 2.2 The Extended Transition Function $\hat{\delta}$

The base transition function $\delta$ handles only **one** input symbol. To reason about a full string $w = w_1 w_2 \cdots w_n$, we extend $\delta$ to $\hat{\delta} : Q \times \Sigma^* \rightarrow Q$ recursively:

$$
\hat{\delta}(q, \varepsilon) = q
$$

$$
\hat{\delta}(q, wa) = \delta(\hat{\delta}(q, w), a)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the recursion:** "To process string $wa$ from state $q$: first process $w$ to reach some intermediate state, then read $a$ to move to the final state." This is the **inductive** definition KTU examiners love to test.

**Language accepted by DFA $M$:**

$$
L(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^* \mid \hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \in F\}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A DFA accepts a string $w$ if and only if the **extended transition** from $q_0$ on $w$ ends in an accepting state. This is the **acceptance criterion** every KTU numerical must verify.

---

## 2.3 Formal Properties of DFAs (High-Yield Theorems)

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently asks 7-mark derivations and 14-mark design problems. Memorize these properties:

- **Uniqueness of computation path:** For any string $w \in \Sigma^*$, there is **exactly one** sequence of states the DFA traverses. This is what makes DFA simulation $O(\vert w \vert)$ — linear time.
- **Closure under complement:** If $L$ is regular, then $\overline{L} = \Sigma^* \setminus L$ is also regular. (Construct a new DFA by swapping accepting and non-accepting states.)
- **Closure under union, intersection, concatenation, Kleene star:** Regular languages form a **Boolean algebra** closed under all standard set/string operations.
- **Decision problems are decidable:** Given a DFA $M$ and a string $w$, the question "$w \in L(M)$?" is decidable in $O(\vert Q \vert \cdot \vert w \vert)$ time. Emptiness, finiteness, and equivalence of two DFAs are also decidable.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formal Statement | Key Notes |
|---|---------|------------------|-----------|
| 1 | DFA Definition | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ | 5-tuple; $Q, \Sigma$ finite, non-empty |
| 2 | Transition Function | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ | Total, single-valued (determinism) |
| 3 | Extended Transition (Base) | $\hat{\delta}(q, \varepsilon) = q$ | Empty string leaves state unchanged |
| 4 | Extended Transition (Step) | $\hat{\delta}(q, wa) = \delta(\hat{\delta}(q, w), a)$ | Recursive definition |
| 5 | Accepted Language | $L(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^* \mid \hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \in F\}$ | The set of accepted strings |
| 6 | Number of Distinct DFAs over $\Sigma$ | $2^{\vert Q \vert} \cdot \vert Q \vert^{\vert Q \vert \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert}$ | All possible 5-tuples |
| 7 | Minimum DFA Size | Myhill–Nerode equivalence classes | Unique up to isomorphism |
| 8 | Regular Language Closure | $\mathcal{R}$ closed under $\cup, \cap, \neg, \cdot, ^*$ | Boolean algebra |
| 9 | Pumping Lemma (Necessary) | $\forall L \text{ regular} : \exists p \geq 1, \forall w \in L, \vert w \vert \geq p, \exists x, y, z : w = xyz$ | Used to prove non-regularity |
| 10 | Simulation Cost | $O(\vert w \vert)$ per string | Constant work per symbol |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas underpin the design of **lexers** (e.g., Lex/Flex), **regex engines**, **hardware controllers**, and **network protocol state machines**. Every compiler, every search engine, and every embedded controller relies on the determinism and finiteness of this model.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Walkthrough: Processing a String on a DFA

To eliminate all ambiguity, let us rigorously demonstrate the computation of $\hat{\delta}(q_0, w)$ for a generic string $w = a_1 a_2 \cdots a_n$ of length $n \geq 1$.

### Step 1: Initialize
The DFA begins in state $q_0$. The input head points to the first symbol $a_1$.

### Step 2: Inductive Hypothesis
Assume the machine has correctly processed the prefix $a_1 a_2 \cdots a_k$ (for some $1 \leq k < n$) and is now in state $q_k = \hat{\delta}(q_0, a_1 a_2 \cdots a_k)$.

### Step 3: Inductive Step
Read the next symbol $a_{k+1}$. Apply the base transition function:

$$
q_{k+1} = \delta(q_k, a_{k+1}) = \delta(\hat{\delta}(q_0, a_1 a_2 \cdots a_k), a_{k+1})
$$

By the definition of $\hat{\delta}$, this equals $\hat{\delta}(q_0, a_1 a_2 \cdots a_{k+1})$.

### Step 4: Termination
After all $n$ symbols are consumed, the machine halts in state $q_n = \hat{\delta}(q_0, w)$.

### Step 5: Acceptance Decision
The string $w$ is **accepted** if and only if:

$$
q_n \in F \iff \hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \in F
$$

The full unfolded computation for $w = a_1 a_2 \cdots a_n$ is:

$$
\hat{\delta}(q_0, a_1 a_2 \cdots a_n) = \delta(\delta(\cdots \delta(\delta(q_0, a_1), a_2) \cdots), a_n)
$$

$$
\boxed{\hat{\delta}(q_0, w) = \delta^{(n)}(q_0, a_1 a_2 \cdots a_n)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiners often ask you to "trace the computation" of a given string on a given DFA. The above five-step protocol is the **canonical answer template** — write initialization, the inductive trace, the final state, and the acceptance verdict.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: DFA Accepting Strings Ending in "01"

**Problem (KTU-style 14-mark):** Construct a DFA over $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ that accepts all binary strings ending in the substring "01". Formally, $L = \{w \in \{0,1\}^* \mid w \text{ ends with } 01\}$.

### Step 1: Identify the Necessary States

To remember the relevant suffix, we need to track:
- $q_0$: No relevant suffix (start, or last symbol was "1" without preceding "0").

Wait, more carefully:
- $q_0$: Have not just seen a "0" (the last symbol was "1", or we are at the start).
- $q_1$: The last symbol read was "0" (potential start of the suffix "01").
- $q_2$: The last two symbols read were "01" (accepting state).

### Step 2: Formally Define the 5-Tuple

$$
M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)
$$

where:

$$
Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}
$$

$$
\Sigma = \{0, 1\}
$$

$$
q_0 = q_0 \quad \text{(start state)}
$$

$$
F = \{q_2\} \quad \text{(accepting state)}
$$

### Step 3: Build the Transition Table

We compute $\delta(q, a)$ for all $(q, a) \in Q \times \Sigma$:

| State | Input $0$ | Input $1$ |
|:-----:|:---------:|:---------:|
| $\rightarrow q_0$ | $q_1$ | $q_0$ |
| $q_1$ | $q_1$ | $q_2$ |
| $*q_2$ | $q_1$ | $q_0$ |

**Transition function $\delta$ explicitly:**

$$
\delta(q_0, 0) = q_1, \quad \delta(q_0, 1) = q_0
$$

$$
\delta(q_1, 0) = q_1, \quad \delta(q_1, 1) = q_2
$$

$$
\delta(q_2, 0) = q_1, \quad \delta(q_2, 1) = q_0
$$

### Step 4: Verify Acceptance on Sample Strings

**String $w_1 = 1101$ (should be accepted):**

$$
\hat{\delta}(q_0, 1101) = \delta(\delta(\delta(\delta(q_0, 1), 1), 0), 1)
$$

$$
= \delta(\delta(\delta(q_0, 1), 1), 0) = \delta(\delta(q_0, 1), 0) = \delta(q_0, 0) = q_1
$$

Wait — let me redo this step by step:

- Start: $q_0$
- Read $1$: $\delta(q_0, 1) = q_0$
- Read $1$: $\delta(q_0, 1) = q_0$
- Read $0$: $\delta(q_0, 0) = q_1$
- Read $1$: $\delta(q_1, 1) = q_2$

Final state: $q_2 \in F$. **Accepted** ✓

**String $w_2 = 1100$ (should be rejected):**

- Start: $q_0$
- Read $1$: $\delta(q_0, 1) = q_0$
- Read $1$: $\delta(q_0, 1) = q_0$
- Read $0$: $\delta(q_0, 0) = q_1$
- Read $0$: $\delta(q_1, 0) = q_1$

Final state: $q_1 \notin F$. **Rejected** ✓

**String $w_3 = \varepsilon$ (empty, should be rejected):**

$$
\hat{\delta}(q_0, \varepsilon) = q_0 \notin F.
$$

**Rejected** ✓ (correct, since $\varepsilon$ does not end in "01").

### Step 5: Confirm $L(M)$ is the Target Language

For any $w \in \{0,1\}^*$:
- The machine is in $q_2$ after reading $w$ **iff** the last two symbols of $w$ (if they exist) are "01" (or $w = 01$ itself).
- The only way to reach $q_2$ is via the transition $\delta(q_1, 1) = q_2$, which requires the previous state to be $q_1$, which in turn requires the last symbol to be "0".
- Therefore, $L(M) = \{w \in \{0,1\}^* \mid w \text{ ends with } 01\}$ as desired. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Number of Distinct DFAs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a favorite **KTU Part A (3-mark)** question.

**Claim:** The number of distinct DFAs over an alphabet $\Sigma$ with exactly $n$ states is:

$$
N(n, \vert \Sigma \vert) = 2^n \cdot n^{n \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert}
$$

**Derivation:**

We count the number of valid 5-tuples $(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where $\vert Q \vert = n$.

- **Choice of $Q$:** We may fix $Q = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ WLOG (renaming states does not change the DFA up to isomorphism, but for *strict* counting of labeled DFAs, there is exactly 1 choice up to labeling).
- **Choice of $F$:** Any subset of $Q$ may serve as the accepting set. The number of subsets of an $n$-element set is $2^n$.
- **Choice of $q_0$:** Any of the $n$ states may be the start state.
- **Choice of $\delta$:** The function $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ is a total function from a set of size $n \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert$ to a set of size $n$. The number of such functions is $n^{n \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert}$.

Multiplying:

$$
\boxed{N(n, \vert \Sigma \vert) = 2^n \cdot n \cdot n^{n \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert} = 2^n \cdot n^{n \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert + 1}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some textbooks exclude the factor of $n$ (for $q_0$) when the start state is implicit; KTU typically uses the formula $2^n \cdot n^{n \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert + 1}$. Verify with your module professor.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DFA vs. Related Models

| Property | DFA | NFA (Preview) | Regex | CFG (Preview) |
|----------|-----|---------------|-------|---------------|
| Memory | Finite (bounded) | Finite (bounded) | N/A | Stack (unbounded) |
| Transitions per $(q, a)$ | Exactly 1 | 0, 1, or many | N/A | N/A |
| Computational Power | Regular | Regular (subset) | Regular | Context-free |
| Acceptance | Reach final state | Some path reaches final | Matches pattern | Derives string |
| Determinism | Yes | No | No | No |
| KTU Module | **Module 1** | Module 2 | Module 1/2 | Module 3 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 State Transition Diagram — DFA for $L = \{w \in \{0,1\}^* \mid w \text{ ends in } 01\}$

Below is the **complete state-transition diagram** for the DFA constructed in Section 3.2. We use **safe alphanumeric node IDs** (`qzero`, `qone`, `qtwo`) to comply with Mermaid's parser rules.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> qzero
    qzero --> qone : 0
    qzero --> qzero : 1
    qone --> qone : 0
    qone --> qtwo : 1
    qtwo --> qone : 0
    qtwo --> qzero : 1
    qtwo --> [*]

    note right of qzero : Start state\nq0
    note right of qone : "Last symbol was 0"\nq1
    note right of qtwo : Accepting\n"Last two: 01"\nq2
```

**Diagram reading guide:**
- The `[*] --> qzero` arrow marks the **start state** ($q_0$).
- The double circle on `qtwo` (the `[*]` exit after it) represents the **accepting state** ($q_2$).
- Every state has **exactly two outgoing edges** (one for input $0$, one for input $1$) — this confirms **determinism and totality**.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a DFA

The following **Mermaid flowchart** depicts the **runtime architecture** of a DFA processing an input string — equivalent to the conceptual tape-head model used in formal language textbooks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input String w on Tape] --> B[Initialize: read head at position 1, current_state = q0]
    B --> C{End of tape?}
    C -- No --> D[Read next symbol a]
    D --> E[Lookup transition: next_state = delta current_state a]
    E --> F[Update: current_state = next_state]
    F --> G[Move read head one cell right]
    G --> C
    C -- Yes --> H{Is current_state in F?}
    H -- Yes --> I[ACCEPT: w is in L of M]
    H -- No --> J[REJECT: w is NOT in L of M]
```

**Reading the architecture:**
- The **tape** is a read-only, one-dimensional array holding the input string $w$.
- The **read head** advances strictly left-to-right, one symbol per step.
- The **transition lookup** $\delta(q, a)$ is a constant-time table lookup.
- The **final decision** is made only after the entire string is consumed.

> [!TIP]
> This block diagram is the **standard answer figure** KTU examiners expect in 14-mark DFA design problems. Drawing it explicitly earns you **presentation marks** even if your transition table has minor typos.

---

## 4.3 Schematic: DFA as a 5-Tuple Component Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Set Q\nFinite States] --> M((DFA M))
    Sigma[Alphabet Sigma\nInput Symbols] --> M
    Delta[Transition Function\ndelta: Q x Sigma -> Q] --> M
    Q0[Start State q0 in Q] --> M
    F[Accepting States F subset Q] --> M
    M --> LOut[Accepted Language L of M\n= w in Sigma* | delta-hat q0 w in F]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This component map is useful when answering **"Define a DFA"** questions in Part A. KTU examiners award 1 mark per component when the 5-tuple is enumerated cleanly.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA). List all its components with a one-line description of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA) is a 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where:
> 1. **$Q$** is a finite, non-empty set of **states**. (½ mark)
> 2. **$\Sigma$** is a finite, non-empty set of **input symbols** (the alphabet). (½ mark)
> 3. **$\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$** is the **transition function** mapping each (state, symbol) pair to exactly one next state. (1 mark)
> 4. **$q_0 \in Q$** is the **start (initial) state**. (½ mark)
> 5. **$F \subseteq Q$** is the set of **accepting (final) states**. (½ mark)
>
> The term "deterministic" emphasizes that for every $(q, a) \in Q \times \Sigma$, the transition $\delta(q, a)$ yields a **unique, single** state.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State and explain the condition for a string $w$ to be accepted by a DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A string $w \in \Sigma^*$ is **accepted** by a DFA $M$ if and only if the computation, starting from $q_0$ and processing each symbol of $w$ via $\delta$, terminates in a state belonging to $F$. (1 mark)
>
> Formally, using the **extended transition function** $\hat{\delta} : Q \times \Sigma^* \rightarrow Q$:
>
> $$
> w \in L(M) \iff \hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \in F
> $$
>
> **(1 mark)** for the equation. **(1 mark)** for explaining that the computation must process the *entire* string, and that $\hat{\delta}$ is defined recursively by:
> - $\hat{\delta}(q, \varepsilon) = q$
> - $\hat{\delta}(q, xa) = \delta(\hat{\delta}(q, x), a)$ for $x \in \Sigma^*, a \in \Sigma$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **State minimization is NOT required** unless explicitly asked. Do not waste 10 minutes minimizing a 3-state DFA into a 2-state DFA — the marks are for correctness of design, not optimality.
> - Always draw the **state diagram** alongside the transition table. Missing diagrams cost **2–3 marks** in 14-mark design problems.
> - Clearly mark the **start state** (incoming arrow) and **accepting state** (double circle). Examiners explicitly check for these markers.
> - For "trace" sub-parts, **show every step** of $\hat{\delta}(q_0, w)$. Skipping steps loses 1 mark per skipped transition.
> - The keyword **"deterministic"** must be respected: every row of the transition table must have **exactly one entry per input symbol**. Self-loop omissions = -2 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Construct a DFA that accepts all strings over $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$ that contain the substring "aba".**

#### **Part (a) (7 marks): Define the 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ and present the transition table.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify states.** We track the longest suffix of the input read so far that is also a **prefix** of the target substring "aba". This gives the set of states:

- $q_0$: No useful suffix (start).
- $q_1$: Suffix is "a" (matches first character of "aba").
- $q_2$: Suffix is "ab" (matches first two characters).
- $q_3$: Suffix is "aba" (full substring found — accepting state).
- $q_4$: **Dead/trap state** (the substring "aba" will never be completed from here).

**Step 2: 5-tuple definition (2 marks):**

$$
Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4\}, \quad \Sigma = \{a, b\}
$$

$$
q_0 = q_0, \quad F = \{q_3\}
$$

**Step 3: Transition table (5 marks):**

We compute $\delta(q, x)$ for every state and symbol:

| State | Input $a$ | Input $b$ | Meaning |
|:-----:|:---------:|:---------:|:-------:|
| $\rightarrow q_0$ | $q_1$ | $q_0$ | No progress / "a" seen |
| $q_1$ | $q_1$ | $q_2$ | "a" or "ab" |
| $q_2$ | $q_3$ | $q_0$ | "ab" or failure |
| $*q_3$ | $q_3$ | $q_3$ | Accepting (absorbing) |
| $q_4$ | $q_4$ | $q_4$ | Dead state |

**Verification of non-trivial transitions:**

- $\delta(q_0, a) = q_1$: After reading "a", the longest matching prefix of "aba" that is a suffix of "a" is "a" itself.
- $\delta(q_1, b) = q_2$: After "a" then "b", the suffix "ab" matches the first two chars of "aba".
- $\delta(q_2, a) = q_3$: After "ab" then "a", the full substring "aba" is found.
- $\delta(q_2, b) = q_0$: After "abb", no suffix is a prefix of "aba" except the empty string.
- $\delta(q_3, a) = \delta(q_3, b) = q_3$: Once "aba" has appeared, the string will always contain it (accepting state is absorbing).

#### **Part (b) (7 marks): Draw the state diagram and trace the string $w = aababa$ on this DFA.**

**Model Solution:**

**State Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> st0
    st0 --> st1 : a
    st0 --> st0 : b
    st1 --> st1 : a
    st1 --> st2 : b
    st2 --> st3 : a
    st2 --> st0 : b
    st3 --> st3 : a
    st3 --> st3 : b
    st3 --> [*]
```

**Trace of $w = aababa$ (4 marks):**

We compute $\hat{\delta}(q_0, aababa)$ step by step:

$$
\hat{\delta}(q_0, a) = \delta(q_0, a) = q_1
$$

$$
\hat{\delta}(q_0, aa) = \delta(q_1, a) = q_1
$$

$$
\hat{\delta}(q_0, aab) = \delta(q_1, b) = q_2
$$

$$
\hat{\delta}(q_0, aaba) = \delta(q_2, a) = q_3
$$

$$
\hat{\delta}(q_0, aabab) = \delta(q_3, b) = q_3
$$

$$
\hat{\delta}(q_0, aababa) = \delta(q_3, a) = q_3
$$

**Final state:** $q_3 \in F$. **String accepted** ✓ (which is correct, since "aababa" contains "aba" starting at position 3).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Construct a DFA over $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ that accepts all binary strings whose decimal value is divisible by 3. Show the DFA, transition table, and trace $w = 1001$.**

#### **Part (a) (7 marks): Design the DFA.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Insight — Modulo-3 arithmetic.** The value of a binary string $w$ modulo 3 is captured by the **running remainder** when reading bits from left to right. The recurrence is:

$$
\text{rem}(w \cdot 0) = 2 \cdot \text{rem}(w) \bmod 3
$$

$$
\text{rem}(w \cdot 1) = (2 \cdot \text{rem}(w) + 1) \bmod 3
$$

**Step 2: States = $\{0, 1, 2\}$ (the three possible remainders).**

- $q_0$: Remainder 0 (accepting).
- $q_1$: Remainder 1.
- $q_2$: Remainder 2.

**Step 3: 5-tuple (2 marks):**

$$
M = (\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, \{0, 1\}, \delta, q_0, \{q_0\})
$$

**Step 4: Transition table (5 marks):**

We compute each transition using the recurrence above.

| State | Input $0$ (i.e., $\cdot 2 \bmod 3$) | Input $1$ (i.e., $\cdot 2 + 1 \bmod 3$) |
|:-----:|:-----------------------------------:|:----------------------------------------:|
| $\rightarrow *q_0$ | $q_0$ (since $2 \cdot 0 = 0$) | $q_1$ (since $2 \cdot 0 + 1 = 1$) |
| $q_1$ | $q_2$ (since $2 \cdot 1 = 2$) | $q_0$ (since $2 \cdot 1 + 1 = 3 \equiv 0$) |
| $q_2$ | $q_1$ (since $2 \cdot 2 = 4 \equiv 1$) | $q_2$ (since $2 \cdot 2 + 1 = 5 \equiv 2$) |

**Compact $\delta$ definition:**

$$
\delta(q_0, 0) = q_0, \quad \delta(q_0, 1) = q_1
$$

$$
\delta(q_1, 0) = q_2, \quad \delta(q_1, 1) = q_0
$$

$$
\delta(q_2, 0) = q_1, \quad \delta(q_2, 1) = q_2
$$

#### **Part (b) (7 marks): Trace $w = 1001$ and draw the state diagram.**

**Trace of $w = 1001$ (4 marks):**

Recall $w = 1001_2 = 9_{10}$, and $9 \bmod 3 = 0$, so the DFA should accept.

$$
\hat{\delta}(q_0, 1) = \delta(q_0, 1) = q_1
$$

$$
\hat{\delta}(q_0, 10) = \delta(q_1, 0) = q_2
$$

$$
\hat{\delta}(q_0, 100) = \delta(q_2, 0) = q_1
$$

$$
\hat{\delta}(q_0, 1001) = \delta(q_1, 1) = q_0
$$

**Final state:** $q_0 \in F$. **String accepted** ✓ (correct: $9$ is divisible by $3$).

**State Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> r0
    r0 --> r0 : 0
    r0 --> r1 : 1
    r1 --> r2 : 0
    r1 --> r0 : 1
    r2 --> r1 : 0
    r2 --> r2 : 1
    r0 --> [*]
```

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: DFA Foundations**

- **Alphabet $\Sigma$** = finite, non-empty set of symbols.
- **String $w$** = finite sequence of symbols; $\vert w \vert$ = length; $\varepsilon$ = empty string.
- **Language $L$** = any subset of $\Sigma^*$; can be infinite.
- **Regular language** = a language accepted by some DFA (equivalently, described by a regex).
- **DFA** = 5-tuple $(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$; all five components are mandatory in your definition.
- **Determinism** = $\delta(q, a)$ is **uniquely defined** for every $(q, a) \in Q \times \Sigma$.
- **Extended transition** $\hat{\delta}$: base case $\hat{\delta}(q, \varepsilon) = q$; recursive case $\hat{\delta}(q, wa) = \delta(\hat{\delta}(q, w), a)$.
- **Acceptance criterion**: $w \in L(M) \iff \hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \in F$.
- **Empty string acceptance**: $\varepsilon \in L(M) \iff q_0 \in F$.
- **Closure properties**: Regular languages are closed under union, intersection, complement, concatenation, and Kleene star.
- **Counting formula**: Number of labeled DFAs with $n$ states over $\Sigma$ is $2^n \cdot n^{n \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert + 1}$.
- **Design pattern**: When designing a DFA, always (1) identify what "memory" is needed, (2) define $Q$ as the set of relevant memory states, (3) build $\delta$ from "what happens when symbol $a$ arrives in state $q$", and (4) include a **dead/trap state** if the DFA must be total.
- **Drawing convention**: Start state = incoming arrow from nowhere; Accepting state = double circle; every (state, symbol) pair must have exactly one outgoing labeled edge.
- **KTU exam tip**: When asked to "design a DFA," present the **5-tuple**, the **transition table**, the **state diagram**, and **at least two verification traces** (one accepted, one rejected).
- **Pitfall to avoid**: Do not confuse DFAs with NFAs in this module — NFAs are a Module 2 topic and have a *different* transition function signature $\delta : Q \times \Sigma_\varepsilon \rightarrow 2^Q$.
- **Real-world link**: Every lexer, regex engine, and digital sequential circuit is a working DFA — recognizing this connection helps with both exam answers and interview questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nondeterministic Finite State Automata (NFA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT419 - INTRODUCTION TO THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nondeterministic Finite State Automata (NFA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of NFA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nondeterministic Finite State Automaton (NFA)** is a mathematical model of computation that generalizes the Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA) by allowing **multiple possible next states** for a given input symbol, and additionally permitting **spontaneous transitions** (ε-transitions) that consume no input.

A NFA is formally defined as a **5-tuple** (a quintuple):

$$M = (Q,\ \Sigma,\ \delta,\ q_0,\ F)$$

Where each component is precisely defined as follows:

- $Q$ : A **finite, non-empty set of states** of the automaton.
- $\Sigma$ : A **finite, non-empty set of input symbols** (the alphabet). Often called the *input alphabet*.
- $\delta$ : The **transition function** that maps a state and an input symbol (or ε) to a *set* of possible next states.
- $q_0 \in Q$ : The **initial state** (start state) — exactly one per NFA.
- $F \subseteq Q$ : The **set of final (accepting) states**. $F$ may be empty in pathological cases, though such NFAs accept no language.

The transition function is the heart of the NFA. Mathematically:

$$\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^{Q}$$

Here, $2^{Q}$ denotes the **power set of $Q$** (the set of all subsets of $Q$). This means that for every pair (state, symbol), the transition function returns an *entire set of states* — and this set can be empty, singleton, or contain many states. This is the **nondeterminism**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from DFA**: In a DFA, $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ returns a *single* state. In an NFA, $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^{Q}$ returns a *set* of states. This single change is what creates the entire theory of nondeterminism.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Reading a Map with Multiple Roads"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Explanation (Real-World Analogy)**: Imagine you are standing at a city intersection, and you want to read a sign that says "TURN LEFT". A **DFA** is like a city where the sign points to **exactly one road** at every intersection — there is no choice, you must follow the indicated road. A **NFA**, however, is like a city where the sign says *"from here you may turn left, go straight, or even teleport to a parallel universe street"* — multiple options exist. The NFA is said to **accept** the journey (the input string) if **at least one of the paths** you could have taken leads to a **destination marked as "ACCEPTED"** (a final state). You do not need to know in advance *which* path is the "correct" one — you only need to know that **a correct path exists**.

This is the essence of **nondeterminism**: the machine *guesses* the right path (or, more formally, explores *all* possible paths in parallel) and accepts if any one branch ends in acceptance.

---

## 1.3 Acceptance of a String by an NFA

A string $w \in \Sigma^*$ is said to be **accepted** by an NFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ if there exists **at least one** sequence of choices of transitions, starting from $q_0$, that consumes all symbols of $w$ and lands in some state belonging to $F$.

Formally, using the **extended transition function** $\hat{\delta} : Q \times \Sigma^* \rightarrow 2^{Q}$:

$$w \in L(M) \iff \hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \emptyset$$

The language **accepted** (or **recognized**) by $M$ is:

$$L(M) = \{\, w \in \Sigma^* \mid \hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \emptyset \,\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024)**: The KTU Module 1 outcome expects students to *define, draw, and analyze NFAs* with and without ε-transitions, compute $\hat{\delta}$, and contrast NFA acceptance with DFA acceptance.

---

## 1.4 What "Nondeterministic" Physically Means

Nondeterminism is a **mathematical abstraction**, not a claim about real hardware. Computationally, a NFA can be **simulated by a DFA** at the cost of exponential blow-up in the number of states (via the **subset construction**). The two models recognize the **same class of languages** — the **regular languages**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Transition Function $\delta$

The transition function $\delta$ is defined on two kinds of inputs:

1. **Symbol transitions**: For $q \in Q$ and $a \in \Sigma$,
$$\delta(q, a) = \text{set of states reachable from } q \text{ on input } a$$

2. **Epsilon transitions (ε-transitions)**: For $q \in Q$,
$$\delta(q, \varepsilon) = \text{set of states reachable from } q \text{ without consuming any input}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **ε-closure**: The set $\delta(q, \varepsilon)$ together with all states reachable via chains of ε-transitions is called the **ε-closure of $q$**, denoted $E(q)$ or $ECLOSE(q)$.

---

## 2.2 The Extended Transition Function $\hat{\delta}$

To reason about strings rather than single symbols, we use the **extended transition function** $\hat{\delta}$. It is defined **recursively** for $q \in Q$, $w \in \Sigma^*$, and $a \in \Sigma$:

**Base case** (empty string):
$$\hat{\delta}(q, \varepsilon) = \{q\}$$

**Recursive case** (consume one more symbol $a$):
$$\hat{\delta}(q, wa) = \bigcup_{p \,\in\, \hat{\delta}(q, w)} \delta(p, a)$$

In words: first compute the set of states reachable on $w$, then for each such state $p$, take the union of all states reachable on the next symbol $a$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **With ε-transitions**, the extended function is modified. Define $\hat{\delta}_\varepsilon$ that also incorporates ε-closure:
> $$\hat{\delta}(q, wa) = \bigcup_{p \,\in\, \hat{\delta}(q, w)} E\!\left(\delta(p, a)\right)$$
> where $E(S) = \bigcup_{s \in S} E(s)$ is the ε-closure of a set $S$.

---

## 2.3 ε-Closure — Formal Definition

For any state $q \in Q$, the **ε-closure** $E(q)$ is defined as the smallest set such that:

- $q \in E(q)$ (the state is always in its own closure), and
- If $p \in E(q)$, then $\delta(p, \varepsilon) \subseteq E(q)$ (any state reachable from $p$ via a single ε-transition is in the closure).

The **ε-closure of a set of states** $S \subseteq Q$ is:

$$E(S) = \bigcup_{q \in S} E(q)$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Meaning / Range |
|---|---|---|
| NFA Tuple | $(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ | 5 components |
| Transition function | $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \to 2^{Q}$ | Maps to a *set* of states |
| DFA Transition (for comparison) | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \to Q$ | Maps to a *single* state |
| Number of possible δ entries | $\vert Q \vert \cdot (\vert \Sigma \vert + 1)$ | Including ε |
| Extended function (symbol step) | $\hat{\delta}(q, wa) = \bigcup_{p \in \hat{\delta}(q,w)} \delta(p, a)$ | For $a \in \Sigma$ |
| ε-closure of $q$ | $E(q)$ | All states reachable from $q$ via ε only |
| String acceptance | $\hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \emptyset$ | Existence of a final state |
| Class of languages | Regular Languages ($REG$) | NFA = DFA in power |
| Subset construction bound | $2^{\vert Q \vert}$ states in equivalent DFA | Worst-case exponential |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Safety Note**: The vertical pipe character `|` was avoided inside table cells; instead, the standard LaTeX notation `\vert Q \vert` is used for cardinality to keep the table well-formed.

---

## 2.5 NFA Without ε-Transitions (Restricted Form)

Some textbooks define a *simpler* NFA where $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \to 2^{Q}$ (no ε-moves). Such NFAs are strictly less powerful *on their own*, but **adding ε-transitions does not increase the class of languages recognized** — every ε-NFA can be converted to an equivalent NFA without ε, which can then be converted to a DFA.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of NFAs

- **Lexical analysis** in compilers: regex-to-NFA conversion (Thompson's construction) is simpler and more uniform than direct DFA construction.
- **Pattern matching** engines (e.g., `grep`, `awk`) often use NFA-based simulation.
- **Model checking** in hardware/software verification often uses nondeterministic automata to encode existential choices.
- **Regular expression engines** internally maintain NFAs because they are **concatenation-friendly** and have a **linear** construction size (versus exponential for DFAs).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — NFA Without ε-Transitions

**Problem**: Define an NFA $M$ over $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ that accepts all binary strings whose **second-to-last symbol is 1**. Write the 5-tuple, draw it, and test the strings $\varepsilon$, `1`, `01`, `10`, `110`, `101`.

### 3.1.1 Construction of the 5-Tuple

We need the machine to "remember" the last two symbols read. Since this is an NFA, we can use nondeterminism to *guess* where the second-to-last symbol occurs.

**Design Idea**: The NFA nondeterministically *guesses* that the current symbol is the second-to-last one. It then verifies the next symbol is anything (since "second-to-last" is followed by exactly one final symbol).

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$

- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$
- $q_0 = q_0$ (start state)
- $F = \{q_2\}$

**Transition table** $\delta$:

| State | On `0` | On `1` |
|---|---|---|
| $q_0$ | $\{q_0\}$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ |
| $q_1$ | $\{q_2\}$ | $\{q_2\}$ |
| $q_2$ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ |

**Reading guide**:
- $q_0$ is a "loop" state — on `0` it stays at $q_0$, on `1` it stays at $q_0$ **or** moves to $q_1$ (nondeterministic guess that this `1` is the second-to-last symbol).
- $q_1$ is the "guessed-second-to-last" state — any next symbol moves to $q_2$ (the accept state).
- $q_2$ is a dead-end final state.

### 3.1.2 Testing Strings

**Test 1**: $w = \varepsilon$

Step 1: Apply base case.
$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, \varepsilon) = \{q_0\}$$

Step 2: Check acceptance.
$$\{q_0\} \cap \{q_2\} = \emptyset$$

Step 3: Conclusion. The empty string is **rejected** (correct — there is no "second-to-last" symbol).

---

**Test 2**: $w = \texttt{"1"}$

Step 1: Compute $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"1"})$.
$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"1"}) = \delta(q_0, 1) = \{q_0, q_1\}$$

Step 2: Check acceptance.
$$\{q_0, q_1\} \cap \{q_2\} = \emptyset$$

Step 3: Conclusion. The string `"1"` is **rejected** (correct — a single `1` has no final symbol after the second-to-last).

---

**Test 3**: $w = \texttt{"01"}$

Step 1: Compute $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"0"})$.
$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"0"}) = \delta(q_0, 0) = \{q_0\}$$

Step 2: Compute $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"01"})$.
$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"01"}) = \bigcup_{p \in \{q_0\}} \delta(p, 1) = \delta(q_0, 1) = \{q_0, q_1\}$$

Step 3: Check acceptance.
$$\{q_0, q_1\} \cap \{q_2\} = \emptyset$$

Step 4: Conclusion. The string `"01"` is **rejected** (correct — second-to-last symbol is `0`, not `1`).

---

**Test 4**: $w = \texttt{"10"}$

Step 1: Compute $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"1"})$.
$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"1"}) = \{q_0, q_1\}$$

Step 2: Compute $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"10"})$.
$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"10"}) = \bigcup_{p \in \{q_0, q_1\}} \delta(p, 0) = \delta(q_0, 0) \cup \delta(q_1, 0) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\}$$

Step 3: Check acceptance.
$$\{q_0, q_2\} \cap \{q_2\} = \{q_2\} \neq \emptyset$$

Step 4: Conclusion. The string `"10"` is **accepted** (correct — second-to-last symbol is `1`).

---

**Test 5**: $w = \texttt{"110"}$

Step 1: Compute $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"1"})$.
$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"1"}) = \{q_0, q_1\}$$

Step 2: Compute $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"11"})$.
$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"11"}) = \bigcup_{p \in \{q_0, q_1\}} \delta(p, 1) = \delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_1, 1) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$$

Step 3: Compute $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"110"})$.
$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"110"}) = \bigcup_{p \in \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}} \delta(p, 0) = \delta(q_0, 0) \cup \delta(q_1, 0) \cup \delta(q_2, 0) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} \cup \emptyset = \{q_0, q_2\}$$

Step 4: Check acceptance.
$$\{q_0, q_2\} \cap \{q_2\} = \{q_2\} \neq \emptyset$$

Step 5: Conclusion. The string `"110"` is **accepted** (correct).

---

**Test 6**: $w = \texttt{"101"}$

Step 1: Compute $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"1"})$.
$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"1"}) = \{q_0, q_1\}$$

Step 2: Compute $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"10"})$.
$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"10"}) = \{q_0, q_2\}$$

Step 3: Compute $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"101"})$.
$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"101"}) = \bigcup_{p \in \{q_0, q_2\}} \delta(p, 1) = \delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_2, 1) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \emptyset = \{q_0, q_1\}$$

Step 4: Check acceptance.
$$\{q_0, q_1\} \cap \{q_2\} = \emptyset$$

Step 5: Conclusion. The string `"101"` is **rejected** (correct — second-to-last symbol is `0`).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — NFA with ε-Transitions

**Problem**: Construct an ε-NFA that accepts the language $L = \{a^n b^m \mid n, m \geq 0\}$. Note that $\varepsilon$ should be accepted (when $n = m = 0$).

### 3.2.1 Construction

- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$
- $q_0 = q_0$
- $F = \{q_2\}$

**Transitions**:
- $\delta(q_0, a) = \{q_0\}$ (loop on `a`)
- $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon) = \{q_1\}$ (spontaneous jump to the `b`-loop section)
- $\delta(q_1, b) = \{q_1\}$ (loop on `b`)
- $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon) = \{q_2\}$ (spontaneous jump to the final state)

### 3.2.2 Testing the Empty String

Step 1: Compute ε-closure from $q_0$.
$$E(q_0) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$$

(because $q_0 \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} q_1 \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} q_2$)

Step 2: For $w = \varepsilon$:
$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, \varepsilon) = E(q_0) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$$

Step 3: Check acceptance.
$$\{q_0, q_1, q_2\} \cap \{q_2\} = \{q_2\} \neq \emptyset$$

Step 4: Conclusion. The empty string is **accepted**, as required.

### 3.2.3 Testing $w = \texttt{"ab"}$

Step 1: Read `a` from $E(q_0) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$.
$$\delta(q_0, a) \cup \delta(q_1, a) \cup \delta(q_2, a) = \{q_0\} \cup \emptyset \cup \emptyset = \{q_0\}$$

Step 2: Take ε-closure.
$$E(\{q_0\}) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$$

Step 3: Read `b` from $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$.
$$\delta(q_0, b) \cup \delta(q_1, b) \cup \delta(q_2, b) = \emptyset \cup \{q_1\} \cup \emptyset = \{q_1\}$$

Step 4: Take ε-closure.
$$E(\{q_1\}) = \{q_1, q_2\}$$

Step 5: Check acceptance.
$$\{q_1, q_2\} \cap \{q_2\} = \{q_2\} \neq \emptyset$$

Step 6: Conclusion. The string `"ab"` is **accepted**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Property | DFA | NFA (no ε) | ε-NFA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transition output | single state | set of states | set of states |
| ε-transitions | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Number of states | minimum often larger | smaller | smallest |
| Equivalent in power | Yes (regular) | Yes (regular) | Yes (regular) |
| Recognition mode | unique path | any path | any path |
| Conversion to DFA | — | Subset construction | ε-closure + subset |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NFA Without ε-Transitions (Accepts "second-to-last symbol is 1")

The following Mermaid state diagram corresponds to Worked Example 1.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q0
    q0: q0 (start)
    q1: q1
    q2: q2 (final)
    q0 --> q0 : 0
    q0 --> q0 : 1
    q0 --> q1 : 1
    q1 --> q2 : 0
    q1 --> q2 : 1
    q2 --> [*]
```

**Reading the diagram**:
- $q_0$ loops on both `0` and `1` (it is a "stay-here-or-guess" state).
- The **extra** arrow from $q_0$ to $q_1$ labeled `1` represents the *nondeterministic guess* that this `1` is the second-to-last symbol.
- Once in $q_1$, any next symbol moves to $q_2$ (the final state).
- $q_2$ is a sink — once accepted, the machine halts.

---

## 4.2 ε-NFA for Language $a^* b^*$

The following Mermaid state diagram corresponds to Worked Example 2.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q0
    q0: q0 (start)
    q1: q1
    q2: q2 (final)
    q0 --> q0 : a
    q0 --> q1 : epsilon
    q1 --> q1 : b
    q1 --> q2 : epsilon
    q2 --> [*]
```

**Reading the diagram**:
- The dashed/epsilon arrows allow the machine to *spontaneously* skip the $a$-loop, the $b$-loop, or even both — this naturally produces $\varepsilon$ as a member of the language.
- The structure mirrors the regular expression $a^* b^*$ directly.

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Architecture: How an NFA is Simulated

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input string w] --> B[Current state set S = q0 closure]
    B --> C{Read next symbol a}
    C --> D[Compute union of delta p a for all p in S]
    D --> E[Apply epsilon closure to result]
    E --> F{End of string}
    F -- No --> C
    F -- Yes --> G[Check intersection with F]
    G --> H{Accept or Reject}
```

**Interpretation**:
- The NFA simulation maintains a **set of currently-active states** (parallel paths).
- On each input symbol, it computes the union of all possible next states.
- After consuming the whole string, the string is accepted **iff** at least one active state is in $F$.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: NFA Inside a Compiler Lexer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Source Code] --> LEX[Lexical Analyzer]
    REG[Regular Expression] --> RE2NFA[RE to NFA Converter]
    RE2NFA --> NFA[Compiled NFA]
    NFA --> SIM[NFA Simulator]
    LEX --> SIM
    SIM --> TOK[Token Stream]
```

**Interpretation**:
- The lexer feeds characters of the source to the NFA simulator.
- The NFA is built once from the regular expression (via Thompson's construction) and reused for every match.
- This is the **standard pipeline** used in real-world tools like `lex`, `flex`, and `re2c`.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q**: Define a Nondeterministic Finite Automaton (NFA). How does it differ from a DFA?

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

A Nondeterministic Finite Automaton (NFA) is a 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \to 2^{Q}$ is a transition function that maps each (state, symbol) pair to a *set* of states (the power set $2^Q$).

**Key differences from a DFA** (1 mark each):
1. **Transition output**: A DFA's $\delta$ returns a single state ($Q$), while an NFA's $\delta$ returns a set of states ($2^Q$).
2. **ε-transitions**: An NFA may include ε-transitions that consume no input; a DFA cannot.
3. **Acceptance semantics**: A DFA accepts if the *unique* path ends in a final state; an NFA accepts if *at least one* of possibly many paths ends in a final state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Both models recognize exactly the regular languages** — they are equivalent in computational power.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q**: What is an ε-closure of a state in an NFA? Give the formal definition.

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

The **ε-closure** of a state $q$, denoted $E(q)$, is the set of all states reachable from $q$ using **zero or more ε-transitions** (i.e., transitions that consume no input symbol).

**Formal recursive definition** (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the base case, 1 mark for the inductive step):

- **Base case**: $q \in E(q)$.
- **Inductive step**: If $p \in E(q)$, then every state in $\delta(p, \varepsilon)$ is also in $E(q)$.

The ε-closure of a *set* of states $S$ is $E(S) = \bigcup_{q \in S} E(q)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Construct an NFA that accepts the language $L = \{w \in \{0, 1\}^* \mid w \text{ contains the substring "01" at least once}\}$. Define it as a 5-tuple, draw the transition diagram, and provide the formal transition table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the NFA constructed in part (a), test whether each of the following strings is accepted or rejected: $\varepsilon$, `"0"`, `"1"`, `"10"`, `"01"`, `"001"`, `"110"`, `"101"`. Show all steps of the extended transition function $\hat{\delta}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Construction (7 marks)

**Design idea**: The NFA uses *nondeterminism* to *guess* the position where the substring `"01"` begins. Once it guesses, it verifies the next two symbols are `0` followed by `1`, then accepts.

**5-tuple definition** (2 marks):

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$

- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$
- $q_0 = q_0$ (start state)
- $F = \{q_2\}$ (final state)

**Transition table** (3 marks):

| State | On `0` | On `1` |
|---|---|---|
| $\to q_0$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | $\{q_0\}$ |
| $q_1$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{q_2\}$ |
| $*q_2$ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ |

(Arrow `→` denotes start; asterisk `*` denotes final.)

**Transition diagram** (2 marks):

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q0
    q0: q0 (start)
    q1: q1
    q2: q2 (final)
    q0 --> q0 : 0
    q0 --> q0 : 1
    q0 --> q1 : 0
    q1 --> q2 : 1
    q2 --> [*]
```

**Explanation of the design**:
- $q_0$ loops on every symbol (so the guess can be delayed indefinitely).
- On reading a `0` at $q_0$, the machine *nondeterministically* chooses to either stay at $q_0$ (ignore this `0`) or move to $q_1$ (guess: this `0` is the start of the substring `"01"`).
- From $q_1$, the machine must see a `1` to reach the accept state $q_2$.
- If at $q_1$ a `0` arrives instead, the machine dies (empty transition set) — that guess was wrong.

---

### Part (b) — Testing Strings (7 marks — 1 mark per string with full step derivation)

We apply the recursive formula:

$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, wa) = \bigcup_{p \in \hat{\delta}(q_0, w)} \delta(p, a)$$

**String 1**: $w = \varepsilon$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \varepsilon) = \{q_0\}$ (base case)
- Acceptance: $\{q_0\} \cap \{q_2\} = \emptyset$ → **Rejected** ✓ (no symbols, no substring)

**String 2**: $w = \texttt{"0"}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"0"}) = \delta(q_0, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\}$
- Acceptance: $\{q_0, q_1\} \cap \{q_2\} = \emptyset$ → **Rejected** ✓ (no `1` follows the `0`)

**String 3**: $w = \texttt{"1"}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"1"}) = \delta(q_0, 1) = \{q_0\}$
- Acceptance: $\{q_0\} \cap \{q_2\} = \emptyset$ → **Rejected** ✓

**String 4**: $w = \texttt{"10"}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"1"}) = \{q_0\}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"10"}) = \delta(q_0, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\}$
- Acceptance: $\{q_0, q_1\} \cap \{q_2\} = \emptyset$ → **Rejected** ✓ (substring would be `"01"`, not `"10"`)

**String 5**: $w = \texttt{"01"}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"0"}) = \{q_0, q_1\}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"01"}) = \delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_1, 1) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\}$
- Acceptance: $\{q_0, q_2\} \cap \{q_2\} = \{q_2\} \neq \emptyset$ → **Accepted** ✓

**String 6**: $w = \texttt{"001"}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"0"}) = \{q_0, q_1\}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"00"}) = \delta(q_0, 0) \cup \delta(q_1, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \emptyset = \{q_0, q_1\}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"001"}) = \delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_1, 1) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\}$
- Acceptance: $\{q_0, q_2\} \cap \{q_2\} = \{q_2\} \neq \emptyset$ → **Accepted** ✓

**String 7**: $w = \texttt{"110"}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"1"}) = \{q_0\}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"11"}) = \delta(q_0, 1) = \{q_0\}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"110"}) = \delta(q_0, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\}$
- Acceptance: $\{q_0, q_1\} \cap \{q_2\} = \emptyset$ → **Rejected** ✓

**String 8**: $w = \texttt{"101"}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"1"}) = \{q_0\}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"10"}) = \delta(q_0, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"101"}) = \delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_1, 1) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\}$
- Acceptance: $\{q_0, q_2\} \cap \{q_2\} = \{q_2\} \neq \emptyset$ → **Accepted** ✓ (substring `"01"` occurs at positions 2–3)

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Construct an ε-NFA that accepts the language $L = \{a, b\}^* a \{a, b\}$ — i.e., the set of all strings over $\{a, b\}$ whose **last symbol is preceded by at least one other symbol** (in other words, strings of length $\geq 2$). Define it as a 5-tuple, draw the diagram, and show the ε-closure of every state. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the ε-NFA from part (a), demonstrate acceptance or rejection of: $\varepsilon$, `"a"`, `"ba"`, `"ab"`, `"bab"`, `"aa"`. Include the ε-closure computations in every step. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Construction (7 marks)

**Design idea**: The ε-NFA uses two stages connected by an ε-transition. The first stage reads *any* string over $\{a, b\}$; the ε-transition moves to the second stage which must read *one more* symbol (which then ends at the accept state).

**5-tuple definition** (2 marks):

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$

- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$
- $q_0 = q_0$ (start state)
- $F = \{q_2\}$ (final state)

**Transitions** (3 marks):

| State | On `a` | On `b` | On $\varepsilon$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\to q_0$ | $\{q_0\}$ | $\{q_0\}$ | $\{q_1\}$ |
| $q_1$ | $\{q_2\}$ | $\{q_2\}$ | $\emptyset$ |
| $*q_2$ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ |

**ε-closures** (2 marks):
- $E(q_0) = \{q_0, q_1\}$ (since $q_0 \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} q_1$, and $q_1$ has no further ε-transitions)
- $E(q_1) = \{q_1\}$
- $E(q_2) = \{q_2\}$

**Transition diagram**:

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q0
    q0: q0 (start)
    q1: q1
    q2: q2 (final)
    q0 --> q0 : a
    q0 --> q0 : b
    q0 --> q1 : epsilon
    q1 --> q2 : a
    q1 --> q2 : b
    q2 --> [*]
```

---

### Part (b) — Testing Strings (7 marks)

The general rule for ε-NFA extended transition:

$$\hat{\delta}(q, wa) = E\!\left(\bigcup_{p \in \hat{\delta}(q, w)} \delta(p, a)\right)$$

**String 1**: $w = \varepsilon$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \varepsilon) = E(q_0) = \{q_0, q_1\}$
- Acceptance: $\{q_0, q_1\} \cap \{q_2\} = \emptyset$ → **Rejected** ✓ (length 0 < 2)

**String 2**: $w = \texttt{"a"}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"a"}) = E(\delta(q_0, a) \cup \delta(q_1, a)) = E(\{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\}) = E(\{q_0, q_2\})$
- $E(\{q_0, q_2\}) = E(q_0) \cup E(q_2) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- Acceptance: $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\} \cap \{q_2\} = \{q_2\} \neq \emptyset$ → **Accepted** ✓

**String 3**: $w = \texttt{"ba"}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"b"}) = E(\delta(q_0, b) \cup \delta(q_1, b)) = E(\{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\}) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"ba"}) = E(\delta(q_0, a) \cup \delta(q_1, a) \cup \delta(q_2, a)) = E(\{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} \cup \emptyset) = E(\{q_0, q_2\}) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- Acceptance: $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\} \cap \{q_2\} = \{q_2\} \neq \emptyset$ → **Accepted** ✓

**String 4**: $w = \texttt{"ab"}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"a"}) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ (from above)
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"ab"}) = E(\delta(q_0, b) \cup \delta(q_1, b) \cup \delta(q_2, b)) = E(\{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} \cup \emptyset) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- Acceptance: $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\} \cap \{q_2\} = \{q_2\} \neq \emptyset$ → **Accepted** ✓

**String 5**: $w = \texttt{"bab"}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"b"}) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ (symmetric to "a")
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"ba"}) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ (from String 3)
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"bab"}) = E(\delta(q_0, b) \cup \delta(q_1, b) \cup \delta(q_2, b)) = E(\{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} \cup \emptyset) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- Acceptance: $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\} \cap \{q_2\} = \{q_2\} \neq \emptyset$ → **Accepted** ✓

**String 6**: $w = \texttt{"aa"}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"a"}) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- $\hat{\delta}(q_0, \texttt{"aa"}) = E(\delta(q_0, a) \cup \delta(q_1, a) \cup \delta(q_2, a)) = E(\{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} \cup \emptyset) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- Acceptance: $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\} \cap \{q_2\} = \{q_2\} \neq \emptyset$ → **Accepted** ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Forgetting ε-closure after every symbol step** is the **#1 cause of lost marks** in ε-NFA problems. After computing $\delta$ on a symbol, you MUST apply $E(\cdot)$ to the result before the next symbol. Many students omit this and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing DFA and NFA transition semantics**: A DFA's δ returns a *single* state, an NFA's δ returns a *set*. Examiners specifically look for this distinction in transition table entries.
> 3. **Marking ε as an input symbol**: Some students list ε in the input alphabet $\Sigma$. The correct convention is that ε is a *transition label*, not an alphabet symbol. The alphabet is *only* the actual input characters.
> 4. **Mis-application of the extended transition function**: The formula is a *union over states* in the previous set, not a *union over symbols*. Get this backwards and the entire string evaluation is wrong.
> 5. **In Part (a) of Question A**, examiners expect explicit mention of the **nondeterministic guess mechanism** in the design. A purely mechanical table without explanation loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Diagrams must clearly show start and final states** (typically with an incoming arrow and a double circle, respectively). Missing these labels costs 1 mark each.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [x] **NFA tuple**: $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ — five components, all required.
- [x] **Key equation**: $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \to 2^{Q}$ — output is a **set**, not a single state.
- [x] **DFA comparison**: DFA has $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \to Q$ (single state, no ε).
- [x] **Acceptance rule**: A string $w$ is accepted iff $\hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \emptyset$.
- [x] **Extended function — base case**: $\hat{\delta}(q, \varepsilon) = \{q\}$.
- [x] **Extended function — recursive case**: $\hat{\delta}(q, wa) = \bigcup_{p \in \hat{\delta}(q, w)} \delta(p, a)$.
- [x] **ε-closure** $E(q)$: set of all states reachable from $q$ via ε-transitions (including $q$ itself).
- [x] **ε-closure of a set**: $E(S) = \bigcup_{q \in S} E(q)$.
- [x] **In ε-NFA simulation**: apply $\delta$ on a symbol, **then** apply ε-closure before the next step.
- [x] **NFA = DFA in power**: Both recognize exactly the **regular languages**.
- [x] **Subset construction bound**: An NFA with $n$ states can be converted to a DFA with up to $2^n$ states (worst case).
- [x] **Nondeterminism = parallel exploration**: a string is accepted if *any* path leads to a final state.
- [x] **Practical use**: regex engines, lexical analyzers, pattern matchers use NFAs internally.
- [x] **Memory trick for the extended function**: "current set of states, take union of all δ moves from each, then take ε-closure (if applicable)" — repeat per symbol.

---

**End of KTU 2024 Module 1 — NFA Notes**

topics.title: NFA with Epsilon Transitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAIT419 - INTRODUCTION TO THEORY OF COMPUTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** NFA with Epsilon Transitions ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of ε-NFA An **ε-NFA (Nondeterministic Finite Automaton with Epsilon Transitions)** is formally defined as a 5-tuple: $$E = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$ where each component carries an extended meaning compared to a standard NFA: - **$Q$** — A finite, non-empty set of **states**. - **$\Sigma$** — A finite, non-empty set of **input symbols** (the alphabet). The symbol $\varepsilon$ (epsilon) is **NOT** part of $\Sigma$. - **$\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^Q$** — The **transition function**, which is defined for every state on every input symbol *and* on the empty string $\varepsilon$. The output is a set of states (a subset of $Q$). - **$q_0 \in Q$** — The designated **start state**. - **$F \subseteq Q$** — The set of **final (accepting) states**. > [!IMPORTANT] > The key distinguishing feature of an ε-NFA is the inclusion of $\varepsilon$ in the domain of the transition function $\delta$. A standard NFA only transitions on actual input symbols from $\Sigma$; an ε-NFA can change state **without consuming any input symbol**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Intuition: The "Free Teleport" Elevator** > > Imagine a building with rooms representing states, and doors between rooms representing transitions on input symbols. A normal NFA is like walking through doors — every step forward **must** consume one letter from a written note (the input string). > > An ε-NFA adds **magical teleporters** (the $\varepsilon$-transitions) between certain rooms. You may, at any time and without reading any symbol, instantly use a teleporter to jump to another room. The string is accepted if you can walk through the doors (reading the input) and use the teleporters (for free) such that you end up in an accepting room exactly when the last letter is read. ### 1.3 Epsilon Closure — The Heart of ε-NFA Since an automaton may take any number of free $\varepsilon$-jumps before or after reading a symbol, we must track the set of states reachable "for free" from a given state. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition — $\varepsilon$-closure of a state $q$:** > > The $\varepsilon$-closure of $q$, denoted **ECLOSE($q$)**, is the set of all states reachable from $q$ using **zero or more** $\varepsilon$-transitions. By convention, $q$ is always in its own $\varepsilon$-closure (zero $\varepsilon$-transitions = stay where you are). Formally, ECLOSE($q$) is the smallest set $S \subseteq Q$ satisfying both: - $q \in S$, **and** - For every $p \in S$, we have $\delta(p, \varepsilon) \subseteq S$. **Extension to a set of states:** For $P \subseteq Q$, $$\text{ECLOSE}(P) = \bigcup_{q \in P} \text{ECLOSE}(q)$$ **Worked Mini-Example:** Suppose $\delta(p, \varepsilon) = \{r\}$ and $\delta(r, \varepsilon) = \{s\}$, and no further $\varepsilon$-transitions exist. Then $\text{ECLOSE}(p) = \{p, r, s\}$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Semantics — How an ε-NFA Processes a String When a string $w = w_1 w_2 \dots w_n$ is fed to an ε-NFA, the machine operates as follows: 1. **Initialisation:** The set of "current possible states" begins as $\text{ECLOSE}(\{q_0\})$ — we may freely teleport from the start state before reading anything. 2. **Read a symbol $w_i$:** From every state $p$ in the current set, we transition on $w_i$ to $\delta(p, w_i)$. The union of all such destinations, taken together, is then closed under $\varepsilon$ (since free teleports may occur after each symbol). 3. **Termination:** After reading the last symbol, the string is **accepted** if the resulting set of current states contains at least one final state from $F$. ### 2.2 The Extended Transition Function $\hat{\delta}$ To capture this precisely, define the **extended transition function** $\hat{\delta} : Q \times \Sigma^* \rightarrow 2^Q$ recursively: **Base case** (empty string): $$\hat{\delta}(q, \varepsilon) = \text{ECLOSE}(q)$$ **Inductive case** ($w = xa$, where $x \in \Sigma^*$, $a \in \Sigma$): $$\hat{\delta}(q, wa) = \text{ECLOSE}\!\left(\bigcup_{p \,\in\, \hat{\delta}(q, w)} \delta(p, a)\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Intuition for $\hat{\delta}$:** It says — "To find all states reachable from $q$ on string $w$, first find all states reachable on the prefix, then take one step on the next symbol from each of those states, and finally close everything under free $\varepsilon$-jumps." The **language accepted** by an ε-NFA $E$ is: $$L(E) = \{\, w \in \Sigma^* \mid \hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \varnothing \,\}$$ ### 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | ε-NFA tuple | $(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ | $\delta$ defined on $Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\})$ | | ε-closure (state) | $\text{ECLOSE}(q) = \text{smallest set } S \text{ with } q \in S \text{ and } \delta(p, \varepsilon) \subseteq S \ \forall p \in S$ | Always includes $q$ itself | | ε-closure (set) | $\text{ECLOSE}(P) = \bigcup_{q \in P} \text{ECLOSE}(q)$ | Distributive over union | | Extended $\delta$ (base) | $\hat{\delta}(q, \varepsilon) = \text{ECLOSE}(q)$ | Empty string jumps freely | | Extended $\delta$ (step) | $\hat{\delta}(q, wa) = \text{ECLOSE}\!\left(\bigcup_{p \in \hat{\delta}(q,w)} \delta(p, a)\right)$ | One symbol + free jumps | | Acceptance | $L(E) = \{ w \in \Sigma^* \mid \hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \varnothing \}$ | Any final state reached | | Equivalence | Every ε-NFA $\equiv$ some NFA (no $\varepsilon$) | Constructive conversion exists | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why ε-NFA matters in engineering:** ε-NFAs arise naturally when constructing automata from regular expressions via Thompson's construction — the standard pipeline used in lexical analysers (lex/flex), pattern matchers (`grep`), and the regex engines of every major programming language. The free $\varepsilon$-jumps elegantly encode the structural concatenation, union, and Kleene-star operations. ### 2.4 Properties and Theorems - **Closure under union, concatenation, Kleene-star:** If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are accepted by ε-NFAs $E_1$ and $E_2$, then $L_1 \cup L_2$, $L_1 L_2$, and $L_1^*$ are also accepted by some ε-NFA. - **Equivalence with NFA:** For every ε-NFA $E$, there exists a standard NFA $N$ (with no $\varepsilon$-transitions) such that $L(E) = L(N)$. The construction is algorithmic and is the cornerstone of the conversion covered in Section 3. - **Closure under reversal, intersection, complement:** Inherited from NFA/DFA theory. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 The ε-Elimination Algorithm (ε-NFA → NFA) Given an ε-NFA $E = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$, we construct an equivalent NFA $N = (Q, \Sigma, \delta', q_0, F')$ as follows: **Step 1 — Compute ε-closure of every state.** For each $q \in Q$, compute $\text{ECLOSE}(q)$ by repeatedly adding states reachable via $\varepsilon$-transitions until no new state is added. **Step 2 — Define the new transition function $\delta'$.** For each $q \in Q$ and each $a \in \Sigma$: $$\delta'(q, a) = \text{ECLOSE}\!\left(\bigcup_{p \,\in\, \text{ECLOSE}(q)} \delta(p, a)\right)$$ In words: "From $q$, first take all free $\varepsilon$-jumps to reach a set of states; from each of those, read the symbol $a$; then take free $\varepsilon$-jumps again." **Step 3 — Determine new final states.** $$F' = \{\, q \in Q \mid \text{ECLOSE}(q) \cap F \neq \varnothing \,\}$$ A state $q$ becomes final in the new NFA if any state reachable from $q$ via free $\varepsilon$-jumps was final in the original ε-NFA. ### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example **Problem.** Convert the following ε-NFA, which accepts the language $L = 0^* 1^*$ (any number of $0$s followed by any number of $1$s, including the empty string), into an equivalent NFA without $\varepsilon$-transitions. **Given components:** $$Q = \{q_0, q_1\}, \quad \Sigma = \{0, 1\}, \quad q_0 = \text{start}, \quad F = \{q_0, q_1\}$$ Transition function $\delta$: $$\delta(q_0, 0) = \{q_0\}, \quad \delta(q_0, 1) = \varnothing$$ $$\delta(q_1, 0) = \varnothing, \quad \delta(q_1, 1) = \{q_1\}$$ $$\delta(q_0, \varepsilon) = \{q_1\}, \quad \delta(q_1, \varepsilon) = \varnothing$$ **Step 1 — Compute ε-closure of each state.** - $\text{ECLOSE}(q_0)$: Start with $\{q_0\}$. From $q_0$, $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon) = \{q_1\}$, so add $q_1$. From $q_1$, $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon) = \varnothing$, stop. Thus: $$\text{ECLOSE}(q_0) = \{q_0, q_1\}$$ - $\text{ECLOSE}(q_1)$: Start with $\{q_1\}$. From $q_1$, $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon) = \varnothing$, stop. Thus: $$\text{ECLOSE}(q_1) = \{q_1\}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $\delta'$ for every state-symbol pair.** **For state $q_0$, symbol $0$:** $$\delta'(q_0, 0) = \text{ECLOSE}\!\left(\bigcup_{p \in \text{ECLOSE}(q_0)} \delta(p, 0)\right)$$ $$= \text{ECLOSE}\!\left(\delta(q_0, 0) \cup \delta(q_1, 0)\right)$$ $$= \text{ECLOSE}\!\left(\{q_0\} \cup \varnothing\right)$$ $$= \text{ECLOSE}(\{q_0\})$$ $$= \{q_0, q_1\}$$ **For state $q_0$, symbol $1$:** $$\delta'(q_0, 1) = \text{ECLOSE}\!\left(\delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_1, 1)\right)$$ $$= \text{ECLOSE}\!\left(\varnothing \cup \{q_1\}\right)$$ $$= \text{ECLOSE}(\{q_1\})$$ $$= \{q_1\}$$ **For state $q_1$, symbol $0$:** $$\delta'(q_1, 0) = \text{ECLOSE}\!\left(\delta(q_0, 0) \cup \delta(q_1, 0)\right)$$ $$= \text{ECLOSE}\!\left(\{q_0\} \cup \varnothing\right)$$ $$= \text{ECLOSE}(\{q_0\})$$ $$= \{q_0, q_1\}$$ **For state $q_1$, symbol $1$:** $$\delta'(q_1, 1) = \text{ECLOSE}\!\left(\delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_1, 1)\right)$$ $$= \text{ECLOSE}\!\left(\varnothing \cup \{q_1\}\right)$$ $$= \text{ECLOSE}(\{q_1\})$$ $$= \{q_1\}$$ **Step 3 — Determine $F'$.** - $\text{ECLOSE}(q_0) = \{q_0, q_1\}$. This contains $q_1 \in F$, so $q_0 \in F'$. - $\text{ECLOSE}(q_1) = \{q_1\}$. This contains $q_1 \in F$, so $q_1 \in F'$. Therefore $F' = \{q_0, q_1\}$ (both states remain final). **Final Equivalent NFA $N$:** $$\delta'(q_0, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\}, \quad \delta'(q_0, 1) = \{q_1\}$$ $$\delta'(q_1, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\}, \quad \delta'(q_1, 1) = \{q_1\}$$ with $q_0$ as start and $F' = \{q_0, q_1\}$. > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** On input "0011" the original ε-NFA can be in $\{q_0, q_1\}$ (since $q_0$ is always final and $q_1$ is final too) — accepted. On input "10" the original ε-NFA cannot reach a final state because the $\varepsilon$-transition only allows $0 \to 1$ ordering — also matches the new NFA's behaviour. ✓ ### 3.3 Conversion Verification Matrix | Original ε-NFA Element | New NFA Equivalent | Reasoning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\delta(q, \varepsilon) = \{p\}$ | Folded into $\delta'$ via $\text{ECLOSE}$ | Free jumps merged with each real step | | $q \in F$ (original final) | $q \in F'$ **iff** $\text{ECLOSE}(q) \cap F \neq \varnothing$ | A state becomes final if a final is reachable free of charge | | $\delta(q, a) = S$ | $\delta'(q, a) = \text{ECLOSE}\!\left(\bigcup_{p \in \text{ECLOSE}(q)} \delta(p, a)\right)$ | Read $a$ from any free-jump neighbour, then jump free again | | $\varepsilon \in \Sigma$ of $E$ | $\varepsilon \notin \Sigma$ of $N$ | $\varepsilon$ is fully eliminated | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 State Diagram — Original ε-NFA for $L = 0^* 1^*$ ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeQ0["q0 FINAL"] nodeQ1["q1 FINAL"] nodeQ0 -- "0" --> nodeQ0 nodeQ0 -. "epsilon" .-> nodeQ1 nodeQ1 -- "1" --> nodeQ1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram:** A solid arrow `-->` denotes a transition consuming an input symbol; a dotted arrow `-.->` denotes an $\varepsilon$-transition (free teleport). Double-ringed or explicitly labelled "FINAL" nodes are accepting states. The self-loop on $q_0$ labelled `0` means any number of $0$s can be read in state $q_0$. The $\varepsilon$-arrow allows the machine to silently slip into $q_1$, after which only $1$s may be read. ### 4.2 State Diagram — Converted NFA (No Epsilon) ```mermaid flowchart LR stateQ0["q0 FINAL"] stateQ1["q1 FINAL"] stateQ0 -- "0" --> stateQ0 stateQ0 -- "0" --> stateQ1 stateQ1 -- "1" --> stateQ1 stateQ1 -- "0" --> stateQ0 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation from the diagram:** Compare the two diagrams carefully. The $\varepsilon$-arrow in the original has been *baked into* the symbol transitions of the new NFA. For example, the original had a free jump $q_0 \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} q_1$ plus a self-loop $q_0 \xrightarrow{0} q_0$. The new NFA on reading `0` from $q_0$ can go to **both** $q_0$ and $q_1$ — effectively replicating the same behaviour without any free teleport. ### 4.3 Functional Architecture — ε-Elimination Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR inputA["Input epsilon NFA
Q Sigma delta q0 F"] stepA["Step 1
Compute ECLOSE of every state"] stepB["Step 2
Build new delta-prime using closure-union rule"] stepC["Step 3
Mark finals whose ECLOSE meets original F"] outputA["Output NFA
Q Sigma delta-prime q0 F-prime"] inputA --> stepA stepA --> stepB stepB --> stepC stepC --> outputA ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block-level interpretation:** The pipeline reads the original ε-NFA description, performs three deterministic transformations, and emits a clean NFA whose language is identical to the original. Every step is purely mechanical and can be hand-executed in an exam or automated in software. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define an ε-NFA. How does it differ from a standard NFA? **Model Answer (3 marks):** An ε-NFA is a 5-tuple $E = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where the transition function is $$\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^Q$$ The crucial difference from a standard NFA is that $\delta$ is defined on the **augmented input set** $\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$, allowing the automaton to change state on the **empty string** without consuming any input symbol. A standard NFA can only transition on actual symbols from $\Sigma$. Both are non-deterministic; the new feature is the silent $\varepsilon$-move. *(Mark split: 1 mark for formal 5-tuple, 1 mark for mentioning $\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$ in the domain, 1 mark for stating the silent-jump consequence.)* --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** What is the $\varepsilon$-closure of a state? Compute $\text{ECLOSE}(q_0)$ for the ε-NFA where $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon) = \{q_1, q_2\}$, $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon) = \{q_3\}$, and no other $\varepsilon$-transitions exist. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **$\varepsilon$-closure** of a state $q$ is the set of all states reachable from $q$ using zero or more $\varepsilon$-transitions. It always includes $q$ itself. **Computation:** - Begin with $\{q_0\}$. - Add $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon) = \{q_1, q_2\}$, so the set becomes $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$. - Add $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon) = \{q_3\}$, so the set becomes $\{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3\}$. - Check $q_2$ and $q_3$: neither has an $\varepsilon$-transition. Stop. $$\boxed{\text{ECLOSE}(q_0) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3\}}$$ *(Mark split: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for correct closure computation.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]` **(a)** Construct an ε-NFA for the regular expression $R = (0 + 1)^* 0 1$ using Thompson's construction idea, and clearly mark all states and transitions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Convert the following ε-NFA into an equivalent NFA without $\varepsilon$-transitions. | State | $0$ | $1$ | $\varepsilon$ | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $\rightarrow q_0$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | $\{q_0\}$ | $\varnothing$ | | $q_1$ | $\varnothing$ | $\{q_2\}$ | $\{q_3\}$ | | $*q_2$ | $\varnothing$ | $\varnothing$ | $\varnothing$ | | $q_3$ | $\varnothing$ | $\varnothing$ | $\{q_2\}$ | (*$\rightarrow$ marks start, $*$ marks final*) **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution A(a) — Model Answer** Thompson's construction builds one sub-NFA per regex sub-expression and joins them with $\varepsilon$-transitions. ```mermaid flowchart LR stA(("new q0")) stB["q1"] stC(("q2")) stD["q3"] stE(("q4 FINAL")) stF(("new q5")) stG["q6"] stH(("q7")) stI(("q8 FINAL")) stA -- "epsilon" --> stB stB -- "epsilon" --> stC stC -- "epsilon" --> stF stA -- "epsilon" --> stF stF -- "epsilon" --> stG stF -- "epsilon" --> stH stG -- "0" --> stC stH -- "1" --> stE stC -- "0" --> stC stC -- "1" --> stC ``` **Description of the constructed ε-NFA:** - **Start state $q_0$.** Two $\varepsilon$-transitions leave $q_0$: one to the "loop" sub-NFA (which reads any number of $0$s and $1$s) and one that bypasses the loop (for the empty iteration of $*$). - **Loop sub-NFA** at $q_1$–$q_2$: self-loop on both $0$ and $1$, then $\varepsilon$-jump back to the bypass point and continue into the suffix. - **Suffix** $q_3 \xrightarrow{0} q_4 \xrightarrow{1} q_5$ (final), where $q_5$ is the unique accepting state.
