```mermaid
    REJ["q reject (Halt and Reject)"]

    TAPE_BLOCK <--> HEAD
    HEAD -- "current symbol x" --> CTRL
    CTRL -- "current state q" --> STATEREG
    CTRL -- "delta(q, x)" --> TFDELTA
    TFDELTA -- "next state q-prime" --> STATEREG
    TFDELTA -- "new symbol y" --> HEAD
    TFDELTA -- "direction L or R" --> HEAD
    STATEREG -. "halt signal" .-> ACC
    STATEREG -. "halt signal" .-> REJ
```

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram — Worked Example Schematic

The following Mermaid state diagram shows the architecture of the "Replace-a-with-c" machine derived in Section 3.2.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start(("q0 START"))
    loop1(("q0 SCAN RIGHT"))
    accept(("q accept"))

    start -- "a, c, R" --> loop1
    start -- "b, b, R" --> loop1
    start -- "blank, blank, R" --> accept
    loop1 -- "a, c, R" --> loop1
    loop1 -- "b, b, R" --> loop1
    loop1 -- "blank, blank, R" --> accept
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each arrow is labeled as `read-symbol, write-symbol, direction`. For example, "a, c, R" means: read 'a', write 'c', move right.

## 4.3 Configuration-to-Configuration Flow (Derivation Trace)

The following Mermaid flowchart traces the exact computational path for the input string `"ab"` in the example machine from Section 3.2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cfg0["C0: q0 a b"]
    cfg1["C1: c q0 b"]
    cfg2["C2: c b q0"]
    cfg3["C3: c b q-accept"]
    halt["ACCEPT — Output: c b"]

    cfg0 -- "delta(q0, a) = q0, c, R" --> cfg1
    cfg1 -- "delta(q0, b) = q0, b, R" --> cfg2
    cfg2 -- "delta(q0, blank) = q-accept, blank, R" --> cfg3
    cfg3 --> halt
```

## 4.4 Component Interaction Table

| Component | Function | Connects To |
|---|---|---|
| Tape | Stores symbols, read/write accessed | Read/Write Head |
| Read/Write Head | Reads 1 symbol, writes 1 symbol, moves L/R | Tape, Finite Control |
| Finite Control | Decides next action using $\delta$ | Head, State Register |
| State Register | Stores $q \in Q$ | Finite Control |
| Transition Function $\delta$ | The "program" of the TM | Receives (q, x), emits (q', y, D) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above use purely alphanumeric identifiers (e.g., `cell1`, `cfg0`, `start`) prefixed with letters to avoid reserved keyword conflicts. Labels contain only raw uppercase text and symbols, ensuring clean rendering.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Define a Turing Machine formally. List all seven components of a Turing Machine.**

**Model Answer:**

A Turing Machine is a 7-tuple defined as:
$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, q_{accept}, q_{reject})$$

The seven components are:
1. **$Q$** — Finite set of states.
2. **$\Sigma$** — Finite input alphabet; $\sqcup \notin \Sigma$.
3. **$\Gamma$** — Finite tape alphabet; $\Sigma \subseteq \Gamma$ and $\sqcup \in \Gamma$.
4. **$\delta$** — Transition function: $Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$.
5. **$q_0 \in Q$** — Start state.
6. **$q_{accept} \in Q$** — Accept (halting) state.
7. **$q_{reject} \in Q$** — Reject (halting) state.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: What is a configuration (Instantaneous Description) of a Turing Machine? Give an example.**

**Model Answer:**

A **configuration** of a TM is a complete snapshot of the machine at an instant of time, denoted by:
$$u \, q \, v$$

where $u, v \in \Gamma^*$ are strings on the tape and $q \in Q$ is the current state. The first symbol of $v$ is the symbol currently scanned by the read/write head.

**Example:** If a TM in state $q_2$ is scanning the third symbol of a tape containing `0110`, the configuration is:
$$01 \, q_2 \, 10$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> A common pitfall in TM definition questions is **forgetting to specify the blank symbol $\sqcup$** in $\Gamma$ but **not in $\Sigma$**. Also, students often confuse the **transition function's signature** — it outputs a **triple** $(q', y, D)$, not a pair. Memorize the 7-tuple and the exact signature of $\delta$ to avoid losing 1–2 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Define a Turing Machine formally. Explain each component of the 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, q_{accept}, q_{reject})$ with appropriate notation. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Consider a Turing Machine $M$ with the following transition function:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, 0) &= (q_0, 0, R) \\
\delta(q_0, 1) &= (q_1, 1, R) \\
\delta(q_1, 0) &= (q_1, 0, R) \\
\delta(q_1, 1) &= (q_1, 1, R) \\
\delta(q_1, \sqcup) &= (q_{accept}, \sqcup, R)
\end{aligned}
$$

Trace the computation of $M$ on input `"01"` and determine whether it accepts or rejects. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A **Turing Machine** is a 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, q_{accept}, q_{reject})$ where:

1. **$Q$** is a finite, non-empty set of **states**. The set includes the start state and the two halting states.
2. **$\Sigma$** is the **input alphabet** — a finite set of input symbols. The blank symbol $\sqcup$ is **not** a member of $\Sigma$.
3. **$\Gamma$** is the **tape alphabet** — a finite set of symbols that may appear on the tape. We require $\Sigma \subseteq \Gamma$ and $\sqcup \in \Gamma$.
4. **$\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$** is the **transition function**. For each pair $(q, x)$ of current state and scanned symbol, it specifies a triple $(q', y, D)$ meaning: change to state $q'$, write symbol $y$, move the head in direction $D$ (Left or Right).
5. **$q_0 \in Q$** is the **start state**.
6. **$q_{accept} \in Q$** is the **accept state** — entering it halts the machine and accepts the input.
7. **$q_{reject} \in Q$** is the **reject state** — entering it halts the machine and rejects the input.

#### Model Solution for (b):

The machine starts in configuration $q_0 \, 0 \, 1$. We trace step by step:

**Step 1:** Apply $\delta(q_0, 0) = (q_0, 0, R)$:
$$q_0 \, 0 \, 1 \vdash 0 \, q_0 \, 1$$

**Step 2:** Apply $\delta(q_0, 1) = (q_1, 1, R)$:
$$0 \, q_0 \, 1 \vdash 0 \, 1 \, q_1$$

**Step 3:** The head now sees $\sqcup$. Apply $\delta(q_1, \sqcup) = (q_{accept}, \sqcup, R)$:
$$0 \, 1 \, q_1 \vdash 0 \, 1 \, q_{accept}$$

**Conclusion:** The machine halts in the **accept state** $q_{accept}$. Therefore, the input `"01"` is **accepted** by $M$. The language recognized is the set of all binary strings ending (i.e., where the head hits blank) after scanning at least one `1` after a `0`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** Differentiate between the transition function of a Turing Machine and that of a finite automaton. Why is a TM more powerful? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Design a Turing Machine that accepts the language $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$. Specify all components of the 7-tuple. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Aspect | Finite Automaton (FA) | Turing Machine (TM) |
|---|---|---|
| Signature | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ | $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ |
| Memory | None (only current state) | Infinite read/write tape |
| Head movement | None (input read in fixed direction) | Bidirectional (L and R) |
| Rewrite capability | Cannot modify input | Can erase and write new symbols |
| Halting | Always halts | **May not halt** |
| Power | Regular languages | Recursively enumerable languages |

**Why a TM is more powerful:**
- It has an **infinite writable memory** (the tape) that the FA lacks.
- The head can move in **both directions**, enabling it to revisit input.
- It can **modify the input**, supporting complex iterative procedures.
- As a result, TMs can recognize all regular, context-free, and many more languages, including non-context-free languages like $\{a^n b^n c^n\}$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Intuition:** The TM will match each `a` with a corresponding `b` by repeatedly replacing the leftmost `a` with `X` and the leftmost `b` with `Y`, moving back and forth across the string.

**7-Tuple Definition:**

- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_{accept}\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$
- $\Gamma = \{a, b, X, Y, \sqcup\}$
- $q_0 = q_0$ (start state)
- $q_{accept} = q_{accept}$ (accept state)

**Transition function (high-level):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, a) &= (q_1, X, R) \quad \text{— Mark first } a, \text{ search for matching } b \\
\delta(q_1, Y) &= (q_1, Y, R) \quad \text{— Skip already marked } b\text{'s} \\
\delta(q_1, b) &= (q_2, Y, L) \quad \text{— Found matching } b, \text{ mark it} \\
\delta(q_2, Y) &= (q_2, Y, L) \quad \text{— Move back leftward over Y's} \\
\delta(q_2, X) &= (q_2, X, L) \quad \text{— Move back leftward over X's} \\
\delta(q_2, a) &= (q_0, a, R) \quad \text{— Return to start of remaining } a\text{'s} \\
\delta(q_0, Y) &= (q_0, Y, R) \quad \text{— All } a\text{'s matched; check for extra } b\text{'s} \\
\delta(q_0, \sqcup) &= (q_3, \sqcup, L) \quad \text{— Reached end; verify balanced} \\
\delta(q_3, Y) &= (q_3, Y, L) \quad \text{— Move left over Y's} \\
\delta(q_3, X) &= (q_4, X, R) \quad \text{— Hit leftmost X; all matched correctly} \\
\delta(q_4, Y) &= (q_{accept}, Y, R) \quad \text{— Accept: } n \, a\text{'s = } n \, b\text{'s}
\end{aligned}
$$

The machine accepts iff the input has the form $a^n b^n$ for some $n \geq 1$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — The Formal Definition of a Turing Machine**

- [x] A Turing Machine is a **7-tuple** $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, q_{accept}, q_{reject})$ — memorize verbatim.
- [x] **$\Sigma$** = input alphabet (no blank); **$\Gamma$** = tape alphabet (with blank, contains $\Sigma$).
- [x] The transition function **$\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$** is the "program" of the TM.
- [x] Each $\delta(q, x) = (q', y, D)$ specifies: new state, new symbol, head direction.
- [x] A **configuration** is written as $u \, q \, v$, where $q$ is the current state and the head is on the first symbol of $v$.
- [x] **$q_{accept}$** and **$q_{reject}$** are halting states with no outgoing transitions.
- [x] TMs have an **infinite tape** and a head that can move **both left and right** — this is what makes them more powerful than FA/PDA.
- [x] TMs can **rewrite symbols** on the tape; FA and PDA cannot.
- [x] A TM that halts on every input (accept or reject) recognizes a **Recursive (Decidable)** language.
- [x] A TM that halts and accepts on all in-language strings (but may loop on others) recognizes a **Recursively Enumerable** language.
- [x] The machine starts in configuration $q_0 \, w$ for input $w$.
- [x] **Common pitfall:** Do not forget the constraint $\sqcup \in \Gamma$ but $\sqcup \notin \Sigma$.
- [x] **Common pitfall:** A deterministic TM has exactly one transition per (state, symbol) pair.
- [x] **Church-Turing Thesis:** Any function computable by any algorithmic process is computable by a TM.
- [x] The TM underpins all of computability theory, complexity classes, and compiler design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples of Turing Machines - Turing Machines as Language Acceptors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT419 - INTRODUCTION TO THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Examples of Turing Machines - Turing Machines as Language Acceptors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Turing Machine (TM)

A **Turing Machine** $M$ is formally defined as a **7-tuple**:

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$$

where each component has a precise mathematical role:

| Symbol | Name | Mathematical Role |
|---|---|---|
| $Q$ | Finite set of states | $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n\}$ |
| $\Sigma$ | Input alphabet | $\Sigma \subseteq \Gamma \setminus \{B\}$ |
| $\Gamma$ | Tape alphabet | Finite, non-empty set of tape symbols |
| $\delta$ | Transition function | $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ |
| $q_0$ | Start state | $q_0 \in Q$ |
| $B$ | Blank symbol | $B \in \Gamma$, but $B \notin \Sigma$ |
| $F$ | Set of final (accepting) states | $F \subseteq Q$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The transition function $\delta$ is the *heart* of a Turing Machine. Unlike a finite automaton, $\delta$ allows the head to **write** a symbol on the tape AND **move** Left/Right in a single atomic step. This is what gives the TM its unbounded memory power.

---

### 1.2 Turing Machine as a Language Acceptor — Formal Definition

A string $w \in \Sigma^*$ is **accepted** by a Turing Machine $M$ if, starting from the **initial configuration** $q_0 w$, the machine eventually enters a state $q \in F$ after a finite number of moves.

The **language accepted by $M$** is:

$$L(M) = \{\, w \in \Sigma^* \mid q_0 w \vdash^* \alpha q \beta,\ \text{for some } q \in F,\ \alpha, \beta \in \Gamma^* \,\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Decidable vs. Acceptable (KTU High-Yield Distinction):**
> - A language is **Recursive (Decidable)** if there exists a TM that **always halts** on every input, accepting or rejecting.
> - A language is **Recursively Enumerable (RE)** if there exists a TM that **accepts every string in $L$** but may **loop forever** on strings not in $L$.
> - Every recursive language is RE, but NOT vice versa.

---

### 1.3 Intuition: The Librarian Robot Analogy

Imagine a tiny **librarian robot** standing in front of an infinitely long bookshelf (the tape). The robot can:

1. **Read** the symbol currently in front of it (using its head).
2. **Erase** that symbol and **write** a new one in its place.
3. **Walk** one step left or right along the shelf.
4. **Decide** its next action based on its internal **mood** (state).

The robot has a **finite set of moods** (states), but the **shelf is infinite**. When the robot reaches a special "happy mood" (accepting state), it shouts "**ACCEPT!**" — meaning the input string is in the language. If it ever reaches a "sad mood" (rejecting state) or has no rule to follow, it halts with "**REJECT!**"

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** A TM does NOT need to halt on rejecting inputs to be a *valid language acceptor*. But for the language to be **decidable**, halting on ALL inputs (accept or reject) is mandatory. This subtle distinction is a frequent 7-mark question in KTU papers.

---

### 1.4 Acceptance Criteria — The Three Halting Conditions

A TM halts in exactly one of three ways:

1. **Accept Halting:** Enters a state $q \in F$ → string is accepted.
2. **Reject Halting:** Enters a state $q \notin F$ where $\delta(q, X)$ is undefined → string is rejected.
3. **Infinite Loop:** No halt occurs → for RE machines, this is treated as non-acceptance; for recursive machines, this is forbidden.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write "TM always halts" for any language acceptor. This is WRONG. A language acceptor need not halt on rejection — only an **acceptor for a recursive language** must halt on every input.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Semantics — Step-by-Step Logic

A TM operates on **configurations (Instantaneous Descriptions)**. A configuration is a string of the form:

$$\alpha\ q\ \beta$$

where:
- $q$ is the current state
- The head points to the **first symbol** of $\beta$ (or to $B$ if $\beta = \varepsilon$)
- $\alpha$ is the tape content strictly to the **left** of the head

A single move $C_1 \vdash C_2$ is governed by:

$$\text{If } \delta(q, X) = (p, Y, D),\ \text{then } \alpha\ q\ X\beta \vdash \alpha\ Y\ p\ \beta \text{ for } D = R$$
$$\text{If } \delta(q, X) = (p, Y, D),\ \text{then } \alpha\ Z\ q\ X\beta \vdash \alpha\ p\ Z\ Y\beta \text{ for } D = L$$

where $Z$ is the symbol immediately to the left of the head before the move.

---

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind TM Power — Breaking Down the Logic

- **Why an infinite tape?** Real computers have finite RAM, but the TM is a *theoretical* model. An infinite tape allows **unbounded memory**, capturing any computation.
- **Why a single head?** A single head keeps the model *minimal* — adding multiple heads does not increase the class of languages recognized (Church-Turing Thesis).
- **Why read AND write?** Read-only models are weaker. Writing allows the TM to **mark symbols** it has processed (essential for matching $a^n b^n$).
- **Why left/right moves only?** Diagonal moves can be simulated with multiple L/R steps. Restricting to L/R keeps the model elegant without losing power.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Notation | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Configuration | $q_0 w$ | TM starts at $q_0$, head on first symbol of $w$ | All TM examples |
| Move Relation | $\vdash$ | One computational step | Trace derivations |
| Reachability | $\vdash^*$ | Zero or more steps | Acceptance proofs |
| Language Accepted | $L(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^* \mid q_0 w \vdash^* \alpha q \beta,\ q \in F\}$ | Set of accepted strings | All proofs |
| Blank Symbol | $B$ | Default empty cell | Tape representation |
| String Length | $\vert w \vert$ or $\mid w \mid$ | Number of input symbols | Complexity notes |
| Configuration Length | $\vert \alpha q \beta \vert$ | Total cells occupied | Trace problems |
| Halting Time | $T(n)$ | Max steps on input of length $n$ | Time complexity |
| Space Used | $S(n)$ | Max tape cells touched (excluding input) | Space complexity |
| Acceptance State | $q_f \in F$ | Final/halting-accept state | State diagrams |
| RE Language | $\exists\, M : L = L(M)$ | Some TM accepts $L$ | Decidability proofs |
| Recursive Language | $\exists\, M : L = L(M)$ and $M$ halts always | TM decides $L$ | Decidability proofs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Strategy:** Always use $\mid w \mid$ (not $\vert w \vert$ or $\vert w \vert$) inside markdown tables. Reserve $\vert \cdot \vert$ for clear, inline LaTeX usage outside tables to avoid syntax breakage.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The TM is not just a theoretical toy. It forms the foundation of:

- **Compiler Design:** Decidability of program equivalence, type checking.
- **Formal Verification:** Model checking safety-critical systems (aircraft, medical devices).
- **Cryptography:** Computational complexity of breaking ciphers (NP, NP-complete classes).
- **Operating Systems:** Process termination analysis, deadlock detection.
- **Algorithm Design:** Understanding intrinsic limits of computation (e.g., Halting Problem).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Application Question Hook:** When asked "Why is the Halting Problem important in software engineering?", answer: *Because there is no general algorithm that can determine, for every possible program and input, whether the program will terminate or loop forever. This proves automatic bug-finders are fundamentally limited.*

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Example 1 — TM for $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$

This is the **canonical KTU example** for TM as language acceptor. The language consists of one or more $a$'s followed by an equal number of $b$'s.

#### 3.1.1 Design Intuition (The Algorithm)

We use a **"mark-and-match"** strategy:

1. Start at the leftmost symbol.
2. Mark the leftmost unmarked $a$ by replacing it with $X$.
3. Move right to find the leftmost unmarked $b$, replace it with $Y$.
4. Move back left to the start and repeat.
5. When all $a$'s are marked, scan right: if only $Y$'s and $B$ remain, accept; otherwise reject.

#### 3.1.2 Formal 7-Tuple Definition

$$M_1 = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$$

- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_f\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$
- $\Gamma = \{a, b, X, Y, B\}$
- $q_0 = q_0$
- $F = \{q_f\}$

#### 3.1.3 Complete Transition Function $\delta$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, a) &= (q_1, X, R) \\
\delta(q_1, a) &= (q_1, a, R) \\
\delta(q_1, Y) &= (q_1, Y, R) \\
\delta(q_1, b) &= (q_2, Y, L) \\
\delta(q_2, Y) &= (q_2, Y, L) \\
\delta(q_2, a) &= (q_2, a, L) \\
\delta(q_2, X) &= (q_0, X, R) \\
\delta(q_0, Y) &= (q_3, Y, R) \\
\delta(q_3, Y) &= (q_3, Y, R) \\
\delta(q_3, B) &= (q_f, B, R)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.1.4 Exhaustive Trace on Input $w = aabb$

We trace every step, showing the current configuration after each move. The head position is marked by the state symbol $q_i$.

**Step 0 (Initial):** The tape begins with the input followed by blanks.

$$q_0\ a\ a\ b\ b\ B$$

**Step 1:** Apply $\delta(q_0, a) = (q_1, X, R)$ — mark $a$ as $X$, move right.

$$X\ q_1\ a\ b\ b\ B$$

**Step 2:** Apply $\delta(q_1, a) = (q_1, a, R)$ — skip the second $a$.

$$X\ a\ q_1\ b\ b\ B$$

**Step 3:** Apply $\delta(q_1, b) = (q_2, Y, L)$ — mark $b$ as $Y$, turn back left.

$$X\ a\ q_2\ Y\ b\ B$$

**Step 4:** Apply $\delta(q_2, a) = (q_2, a, L)$ — move left over $a$.

$$X\ q_2\ a\ Y\ b\ B$$

**Step 5:** Apply $\delta(q_2, X) = (q_0, X, R)$ — return to start position.

$$q_0\ X\ a\ Y\ b\ B$$

**Step 6:** Apply $\delta(q_0, a) = (q_1, X, R)$ — mark the second $a$.

$$X\ X\ q_1\ Y\ b\ B$$

**Step 7:** Apply $\delta(q_1, Y) = (q_1, Y, R)$ — skip $Y$.

$$X\ X\ Y\ q_1\ b\ B$$

**Step 8:** Apply $\delta(q_1, b) = (q_2, Y, L)$ — mark second $b$ as $Y$.

$$X\ X\ Y\ q_2\ Y\ B$$

**Step 9:** Apply $\delta(q_2, Y) = (q_2, Y, L)$ — move left.

$$X\ X\ q_2\ Y\ Y\ B$$

**Step 10:** Apply $\delta(q_2, Y) = (q_2, Y, L)$ — move left.

$$X\ q_2\ X\ Y\ Y\ B$$

**Step 11:** Apply $\delta(q_2, X) = (q_0, X, R)$ — return to start.

$$q_0\ X\ X\ Y\ Y\ B$$

**Step 12:** Apply $\delta(q_0, Y) = (q_3, Y, R)$ — all $a$'s marked, switch to verify phase.

$$X\ X\ q_3\ Y\ Y\ B$$

**Step 13:** Apply $\delta(q_3, Y) = (q_3, Y, R)$ — skip first $Y$.

$$X\ X\ Y\ q_3\ Y\ B$$

**Step 14:** Apply $\delta(q_3, Y) = (q_3, Y, R)$ — skip second $Y$.

$$X\ X\ Y\ Y\ q_3\ B$$

**Step 15:** Apply $\delta(q_3, B) = (q_f, B, R)$ — see blank, accept!

$$X\ X\ Y\ Y\ B\ q_f\ B$$

✅ **The string $aabb$ is ACCEPTED.**

---

### 3.2 Example 2 — TM for $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^+\}$ (Palindromes over $\{a,b\}$)

A palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards. The TM must verify that the second half is the reverse of the first half.

#### 3.2.1 Algorithm

1. Read the leftmost symbol $s$.
2. Mark it (replace with blank or special marker).
3. Scan to the rightmost unmarked symbol.
4. Check if it matches $s$.
5. If yes, mark it and repeat.
6. If only markers remain, accept.

#### 3.2.2 Transition Function (Sketch)

States: $q_0$ (start), $q_1$ (find right end), $q_2$ (compare), $q_f$ (accept).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, a) &= (q_1, B, R) \\
\delta(q_0, b) &= (q_1, B, R) \\
\delta(q_1, a) &= (q_1, a, R) \\
\delta(q_1, b) &= (q_1, b, R) \\
\delta(q_1, B) &= (q_2, B, L) \\
\delta(q_2, a) &= (\text{check rightmost unmarked symbol}) \\
\delta(q_2, b) &= (\text{check rightmost unmarked symbol})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Full Trace Omitted for Brevity:** For KTU exams, sketching the algorithm and giving 5–6 key transitions with a partial trace suffices for full 7 marks. Always include a successful trace on a sample input (e.g., $w = abba$).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Sample TM Languages

| Language | TM Strategy | Key States | Time $O(\mid w \mid^2)$? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ | Mark-and-match, sweep | $q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_f$ | Yes |
| $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a,b\}^+\}$ | Match endpoints inward | $q_0, q_1, q_2, q_f$ | Yes |
| $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ | Triple-nested mark-match | $q_0, \ldots, q_5, q_f$ | Yes |
| $L = \{w\#w \mid w \in \{a,b\}^+\}$ | Two-pass with delimiter | $q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_f$ | Yes |
| $L = \{a^{2^n} \mid n \geq 0\}$ | Binary counter | Multiple | No (non-regular, more complex) |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Insight:** Notice that ALL these languages are **non-regular** (cannot be accepted by any DFA/NFA), yet they ARE accepted by TMs. This demonstrates the **strictly greater power** of TMs over finite automata — a guaranteed 3-mark conceptual question.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 State Transition Diagram for $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$

The following Mermaid diagram renders the complete state graph for the TM designed in Section 3.1. Each transition is labeled with the input symbol read, the symbol written, and the head direction.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> startNode
    startNode --> scanA : a / X , R
    startNode --> verifyStart : Y / Y , R
    scanA --> scanA : a / a , R
    scanA --> scanA : Y / Y , R
    scanA --> goBack : b / Y , L
    goBack --> goBack : a / a , L
    goBack --> goBack : Y / Y , L
    goBack --> startNode : X / X , R
    verifyStart --> verifyLoop : Y / Y , R
    verifyLoop --> verifyLoop : Y / Y , R
    verifyLoop --> acceptNode : B / B , R
    acceptNode --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Labels:** In the format `read / write , direction`:
> - `a / X , R` means "Read $a$, Write $X$, move Right"
> - `Y / Y , R` means "Read $Y$, Write $Y$ (no change), move Right"

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This diagram captures the **logical workflow** of any TM-based language acceptor, regardless of the specific language. It maps the interactions between the tape, control unit, and acceptance module.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input String w] --> B[Initialize TM: q0, head at position 0]
    B --> C{Read Current Tape Symbol}
    C --> D[Look up Transition δ in Control Unit]
    D --> E{Transition Exists?}
    E -- No --> F[Halt in Non-Accepting State]
    E -- Yes --> G[Write New Symbol to Tape]
    G --> H[Move Head L or R]
    H --> I{New State q ∈ F?}
    I -- Yes --> J[ACCEPT - w ∈ L(M)]
    I -- No --> K{Any Other Transition Possible?}
    K -- Yes --> C
    K -- No --> L[REJECT or Infinite Loop]
    F --> L
    J --> M[Output: String Accepted]
    L --> N[Output: Rejected / Loop]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mapping Note:** The block `E --> No --> F` represents the case where $\delta(q, X)$ is undefined — the machine halts without acceptance. The block `K --> No --> L` captures the infinite-loop possibility, which is the defining feature distinguishing **RE languages from Recursive languages**.

---

### 4.3 Configuration Trace Visualization for $w = aabb$

This Mermaid graph visualizes the **15-step trace** computed in Section 3.1.4, providing a compact visual summary.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cfg0["q0 a a b b B"] --> cfg1["X q1 a b b B"]
    cfg1 --> cfg2["X a q1 b b B"]
    cfg2 --> cfg3["X a q2 Y b B"]
    cfg3 --> cfg4["X q2 a Y b B"]
    cfg4 --> cfg5["q0 X a Y b B"]
    cfg5 --> cfg6["X X q1 Y b B"]
    cfg6 --> cfg7["X X Y q1 b B"]
    cfg7 --> cfg8["X X Y q2 Y B"]
    cfg8 --> cfg9["X X q2 Y Y B"]
    cfg9 --> cfg10["X q2 X Y Y B"]
    cfg10 --> cfg11["q0 X X Y Y B"]
    cfg11 --> cfg12["X X q3 Y Y B"]
    cfg12 --> cfg13["X X Y q3 Y B"]
    cfg13 --> cfg14["X X Y Y q3 B"]
    cfg14 --> cfg15["X X Y Y B qf B ACCEPTED"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a Turing Machine as a language acceptor. How does it differ from a finite automaton in terms of memory?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Turing Machine (TM)** $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ is said to **accept** a language $L$ if:

$$L(M) = \{\, w \in \Sigma^* \mid q_0 w \vdash^* \alpha q \beta,\ q \in F,\ \alpha, \beta \in \Gamma^* \,\}$$

In other words, starting from the initial configuration $q_0 w$, the TM enters an accepting state $q \in F$ after a finite sequence of moves.

**Difference from Finite Automaton (FA):**

| Aspect | Finite Automaton | Turing Machine |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | None — only current state | Infinite tape (unbounded memory) |
| Tape | Read-only, fixed input | Read/Write, unbounded |
| Head Movement | One-way, left-to-right | Bidirectional (L/R) |
| Power | Regular languages | Recursively enumerable languages |

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for $L(M)$ formula, 1 for comparison)

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Distinguish between Recursive and Recursively Enumerable languages. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Recursive (Decidable) | Recursively Enumerable (RE) |
|---|---|---|
| Halts on every input? | **Yes** — always | Not necessarily — may loop on rejection |
| Decider exists? | **Yes** | Yes (as acceptor), no (as decider) |
| Example | $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ | $L_{Halt} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M$ halts on $w\}$ |

**Key Point:** Every recursive language is RE, but there exist RE languages (like the Halting Problem) that are NOT recursive. (3 marks: 2 for distinction, 1 for examples)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Design a Turing Machine that accepts the language $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$. Draw the state transition diagram and show the trace on input $w = aabb$.**

##### Part (a) — TM Design and State Diagram [7 marks]

**TM Definition:**

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$$

where:
- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_f\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$
- $\Gamma = \{a, b, X, Y, B\}$
- $q_0 = q_0$ (start state)
- $B$ = blank symbol
- $F = \{q_f\}$ (accepting state)

**Algorithm:**
1. From state $q_0$, if input is $a$, mark it as $X$, move right, go to $q_1$.
2. In $q_1$, skip over $a$'s and $Y$'s until $b$ is found. Replace $b$ with $Y$, move left, go to $q_2$.
3. In $q_2$, move left over $a$'s and $Y$'s until $X$ is found. Move right, return to $q_0$.
4. When $q_0$ sees $Y$ (no more $a$'s), go to $q_3$ for verification.
5. In $q_3$, skip $Y$'s; if $B$ is reached, go to $q_f$ and accept.

**State Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram)

**Transition Table:**

| State | $a$ | $b$ | $X$ | $Y$ | $B$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $q_0$ | $(q_1, X, R)$ | — | — | $(q_3, Y, R)$ | — |
| $q_1$ | $(q_1, a, R)$ | $(q_2, Y, L)$ | — | $(q_1, Y, R)$ | — |
| $q_2$ | $(q_2, a, L)$ | — | $(q_0, X, R)$ | $(q_2, Y, L)$ | — |
| $q_3$ | — | — | — | $(q_3, Y, R)$ | $(q_f, B, R)$ |
| $q_f$ | — | — | — | — | — |

##### Part (b) — Trace on $w = aabb$ [7 marks]

The complete 15-step trace is given in Section 3.1.4. The key transitions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_0\, a\, a\, b\, b\, B &\vdash X\, q_1\, a\, b\, b\, B \\
&\vdash X\, a\, q_1\, b\, b\, B \\
&\vdash X\, a\, q_2\, Y\, b\, B \\
&\vdash X\, q_2\, a\, Y\, b\, B \\
&\vdash q_0\, X\, a\, Y\, b\, B \\
&\vdash X\, X\, q_1\, Y\, b\, B \\
&\vdash X\, X\, Y\, q_1\, b\, B \\
&\vdash X\, X\, Y\, q_2\, Y\, B \\
&\vdash X\, X\, q_2\, Y\, Y\, B \\
&\vdash X\, q_2\, X\, Y\, Y\, B \\
&\vdash q_0\, X\, X\, Y\, Y\, B \\
&\vdash X\, X\, q_3\, Y\, Y\, B \\
&\vdash X\, X\, Y\, q_3\, Y\, B \\
&\vdash X\, X\, Y\, Y\, q_3\, B \\
&\vdash X\, X\, Y\, Y\, B\, q_f\, B \quad \text{(ACCEPT)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The TM halts in the accepting state $q_f$ for $w = aabb \in L$. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Design a Turing Machine that accepts the language $L = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a, b\}^+\}$ (palindromes over $\{a,b\}$). Explain the working with an example trace.**

##### Part (a) — TM Design [7 marks]

**TM Definition:**

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$$

where:
- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5, q_f\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$
- $\Gamma = \{a, b, X, B\}$
- $F = \{q_f\}$

**Algorithm:**
1. In $q_0$: read leftmost symbol. If $a$ or $b$, mark as blank $B$, remember symbol, go to $q_1$.
2. In $q_1$: move right to find blank $B$ (rightmost unmarked symbol).
3. In $q_2$: move one step left to check the last symbol.
4. In $q_3$ (for $a$) or $q_4$ (for $b$): verify match with remembered symbol. If match, mark as $X$, move left, return to $q_0$.
5. If only $X$'s and $B$ remain, go to $q_f$.

**Key Transitions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, a) &= (q_1, B, R) \\
\delta(q_0, b) &= (q_1, B, R) \\
\delta(q_1, a) &= (q_1, a, R) \\
\delta(q_1, b) &= (q_1, b, R) \\
\delta(q_1, B) &= (q_2, B, L) \\
\delta(q_2, a) &= (q_3, a, L) \\
\delta(q_2, b) &= (q_4, b, L) \\
\delta(q_3, B) &= (q_0, X, R) \quad \text{(matched }a\text{)} \\
\delta(q_4, B) &= (q_0, X, R) \quad \text{(matched }b\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

##### Part (b) — Trace on $w = abba$ [7 marks]

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_0\, a\, b\, b\, a\, B &\vdash B\, q_1\, b\, b\, a\, B \quad \text{(mark }a\text{, remember }a\text{)} \\
&\vdash B\, b\, q_1\, b\, a\, B \\
&\vdash B\, b\, b\, q_1\, a\, B \\
&\vdash B\, b\, b\, q_2\, a\, B \\
&\vdash B\, b\, q_3\, b\, a\, B \quad \text{(saw }a\text{, but next is }b\text{ — FAIL)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Correction:** The trace reveals a flaw — the machine needs to **mark the rightmost symbol after comparison**, not check immediately. Revised transitions are needed. The correct approach:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_2, a) &= (q_3, X, L) \quad \text{(match }a\text{ with remembered }a\text{, mark as }X\text{)} \\
\delta(q_3, X) &= (q_0, X, R) \quad \text{(return to start)} \\
\delta(q_0, X) &= (q_0, X, R) \quad \text{(skip markers)} \\
\delta(q_0, B) &= (q_f, B, R) \quad \text{(all matched, accept)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Correct Trace on $abba$:**

$$q_0\, a\, b\, b\, a\, B \vdash B\, q_1\, b\, b\, a\, B \vdash B\, b\, b\, q_2\, a\, B \vdash B\, b\, q_3\, X\, B \vdash B\, q_3\, b\, X\, B \vdash q_0\, B\, b\, X\, B \vdash B\, q_0\, b\, X\, B \vdash B\, B\, q_1\, X\, B \vdash B\, B\, X\, q_2\, B \vdash B\, B\, q_3\, X\, X \vdash B\, q_3\, X\, X\, B \vdash q_0\, B\, X\, X\, B \vdash B\, q_0\, X\, X\, B \vdash B\, X\, q_0\, X\, B \vdash B\, X\, X\, q_0\, B \vdash B\, X\, X\, B\, q_f\, B \quad \text{ACCEPT}$$

✅ **Verified:** $w = abba$ is a palindrome, and the TM accepts it.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Blank Symbol $B$:** Always include $B$ in your tape alphabet $\Gamma$. Marks deducted: **1 mark**.
> 2. **Missing Direction (L/R):** Every transition must specify Left or Right. Marks deducted: **0.5 per transition**.
> 3. **Not Drawing the State Diagram:** A state diagram is **mandatory** for 14-mark TM design questions. Marks deducted: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Incomplete Trace:** Tracing only 3-4 steps instead of the full computation. The trace must reach the **accept state** explicitly. Marks deducted: **2 marks**.
> 5. **Confusing Acceptance with Decidability:** Saying "TM accepts $\Leftrightarrow$ TM decides" — they differ! Marks deducted: **1 mark**.
> 6. **No Reject Path:** For recursive languages, you must show what happens for **non-members** too (e.g., $w = abb$ should halt in a non-accepting state).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TM 7-tuple:** Always define $(Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ explicitly.
- **Language Accepted:** $L(M) = \{w \mid q_0 w \vdash^* \alpha q \beta,\ q \in F\}$.
- **Accept vs Decide:** Accept = enter $F$ on strings in $L$ (may loop on others). Decide = always halt.
- **Recursive ⊂ RE:** Every decidable language is acceptable, but not vice versa.
- **Common Languages:** $a^n b^n$, $ww^R$, $a^n b^n c^n$, $w\#w$ are all RE but NOT regular.
- **Mark-and-Match Strategy:** The universal technique for designing TMs for non-regular languages.
- **State Diagram:** Use Mermaid or hand-drawn for KTU exams — every transition needs `read / write , direction`.
- **Trace Format:** Show **every** step from $q_0 w$ to final configuration; circle/highlight the accept state.
- **Head Position:** Always shown by the position of $q_i$ relative to the tape symbols.
- **Blank Symbol $B$:** Always present on the right end of input; used to detect end-of-string.
- **TM Power:** Equivalent to modern computers (Church-Turing Thesis) but with infinite memory.
- **Halting Problem:** Undecidable — proves limits of TM-based program analysis.
- **KTU Mark Split:** TM design = 7 marks (algorithm + diagram); Trace = 7 marks (full step-by-step).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Turing Machines as Computing Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT419 - INTRODUCTION TO THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Turing Machines as Computing Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Turing Machines as Function Computers

A **Turing Machine (TM)** is a 7-tuple formal system, originally conceived by Alan Turing in 1936, that serves as the most general mathematical model of computation. When a TM halts with a specific string on its tape, it is said to **compute a function** mapping its initial input to its final output.

Formally, a Turing Machine is defined as:
$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$$

where:
- $Q$ is a finite set of **states**
- $\Sigma$ is a finite set of **input symbols** (the input alphabet)
- $\Gamma$ is a finite set of **tape symbols** (the tape alphabet), where $\Sigma \subseteq \Gamma$
- $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ is the **transition function**
- $q_0 \in Q$ is the **start state**
- $B \in \Gamma - \Sigma$ is the **blank symbol**
- $F \subseteq Q$ is the set of **final (halting) states**

A TM $M$ is said to **compute a function** $f : \Sigma^* \rightarrow \Sigma^*$ if for every input string $w \in \Sigma^*$, the machine starts with $w$ on the tape (rest being blank), performs a sequence of transitions, and **halts** with exactly $f(w)$ written on the tape.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> A function $f$ is called **Turing-computable** (or **recursive**) if there exists a Turing Machine $M$ that computes it. This notion is the cornerstone for defining what "computable" means in computer science and is directly tied to the **Church–Turing Thesis**.

### 1.2 The Three Halting Conditions of a TM

A Turing Machine's computation with respect to a function $f$ terminates under one of three conditions:

| # | Halting Condition | Mathematical Form | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Successful Halting** | $q_0 w \vdash^* \alpha q_f \beta,\; q_f \in F$ | $f(w)$ is defined and TM halts |
| 2 | **Infinite Loop** | $q_0 w \vdash^* \infty$ | $f(w)$ is **undefined** for this input |
| 3 | **Crash** | $\delta(q, x) = \text{undefined}$ | TM halts abruptly; $f(w)$ **undefined** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A function $f$ is computed by TM $M$ only if $M$ **halts in a final state** for every input in the **domain of $f$**. If $M$ fails to halt, the function value is considered *undefined* on that input — this models **partial recursive functions**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mathematical Vending Machine"

Imagine a Turing Machine as an **ultra-precise vending machine with infinite shelf space**:

- The **input tray** (left end of the tape) accepts a coded product request, e.g., `0 1 1 0` (representing the number 6 in binary).
- The machine reads the request **one symbol at a time**, scanning left-to-right and right-to-left, occasionally erasing symbols or writing new ones — analogous to a chef reading a recipe, doing intermediate calculations, and writing notes.
- The **output tray** (tape contents when the machine halts) presents the answer in the same coded format, e.g., `1 1 1 0` (the number 14).
- If the recipe is invalid (e.g., a function undefined for negative numbers), the machine **runs forever** — like a vending machine that keeps spinning its coil when an out-of-stock code is entered.

This vending-machine analogy is the **intuitive essence** of TM-computed functions: deterministic, mechanical, and capable of simulating any algorithmic process.

### 1.4 Function Notation: $M(w)$ and the Domain Convention

When a TM $M$ halts on input $w$, we write:

$$M(w) = v \iff q_0 w \vdash^*_M u q_f v \text{ and the output is the non-blank string on the tape}$$

- The **domain of $M$**, denoted $\text{dom}(M)$, is the set of all strings on which $M$ halts.
- If $w \notin \text{dom}(M)$, we say $M(w)$ is **undefined** (often written $M(w) = \uparrow$).
- A TM that halts on **every input** computes a **total function**; otherwise, it computes a **partial function**.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU board examinations, you will often see notation like "$M$ computes $f: \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$". Always clarify in your answer whether the function is **total** (defined everywhere) or **partial** (defined only on a subset).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — How a TM Computes a Function

The mechanism by which a TM computes a function involves **four conceptual stages**:

1. **Input Encoding:** The input $w$ is placed on the tape starting at cell 1, surrounded by blanks. For numeric functions, $w$ is often expressed in **unary** ($0^n$ for the number $n$) or **binary** form.

2. **Intermediate Manipulation:** Using its read/write head, the TM traverses the tape, modifies symbols according to $\delta$, and updates its internal state to track progress. Auxiliary symbols (called **markers** like $X, Y, Z$) are used to "remember" processed portions.

3. **Output Formation:** When the TM reaches a final state, the tape contains a possibly modified string. By convention, the **output** is the contiguous non-blank string starting from the leftmost non-blank cell.

4. **Verification of Halting:** The TM is deemed to have computed $f(w)$ **if and only if** it reaches a halting configuration $q_f \in F$. The answer $f(w)$ is then read off the tape.

### 2.2 Standard Notation for TM-Computed Functions

$$f_M(w) = \begin{cases} v & \text{if } M \text{ halts on } w \text{ with output } v \\ \uparrow & \text{if } M \text{ does not halt on } w \end{cases}$$

The collection of all TM-computable functions is denoted:
$$\mathcal{R} = \{ f : \exists M \text{ such that } f = f_M \}$$

This set $\mathcal{R}$ is precisely the set of **(partial) recursive functions**, a foundational result in computability theory.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — TM Function Computation

| Concept | Definition / Formula | Notation | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| TM as Function Computer | $f(w) = v$ if $q_0 w \vdash^* \alpha q_f \beta$ with $v$ on tape | $M(w) = v$ | Halting required |
| Unary Number Representation | $n \leftrightarrow 0^n$ (a string of $n$ zeros) | $\text{unary}(n) = 0^n$ | Common in TM examples |
| Binary Number Representation | $n \leftrightarrow b_k b_{k-1} \ldots b_0$ with $n = \sum b_i 2^i$ | $\text{binary}(n)$ | Compact but harder to manipulate |
| Monus (Proper Subtraction) | $m \dot{-} n = \max(m - n, 0)$ | $m \dot{-} n$ | TM cannot go negative; uses monus |
| Successor Function | $S(n) = n + 1$ | $f(n) = n + 1$ | Simplest non-trivial computable |
| Addition of Two Numbers | $f(m, n) = m + n$ | Encoded as $0^m 1 0^n$ on tape | Use $1$ as separator |
| Multiplication | $f(m, n) = m \times n$ | Encoded as $0^m 1 0^n$ on tape | Nested loops on tape |
| Copy Function | $f(w) = ww$ | Concatenation | Pivotal subroutine in TM design |
| Halting Predicate | $H(M, w) = 1$ if $M$ halts on $w$ | — | **Undecidable** — diagonalization |

### 2.4 Why This Matters — Real-World Engineering Utility

The theoretical framework of TM-computed functions underpins several practical engineering domains:

- **Compiler Design:** The notion of *what is computable* bounds what compilers can do. The uncomputability of the Halting Problem means no perfect bug-detection tool can exist.
- **Programming Language Theory:** The class of **primitive recursive functions** corresponds to programs using only bounded `for` loops; **$\mu$-recursive functions** correspond to programs with unbounded `while` loops.
- **Algorithm Design:** When we say an algorithm is "efficient" or "tractable", we implicitly rely on the TM model with polynomial-time bounds — the very basis of **complexity classes P, NP, and PSPACE**.
- **Formal Verification:** Hardware and protocol verification (used in chip design, cryptography) is fundamentally about checking whether a TM-computed function satisfies a specification.

### 2.5 Critical Design Principles for a TM-Computer

When designing a TM that computes a specific function, the following principles must be observed:

- **The blank $B$** is your "free scratch space" — it can be used as a separator or fresh workspace.
- **Marker symbols** (capital letters like $X, Y, Z$) must be distinct from $\Sigma$ and $B$ to avoid ambiguity.
- The TM must **explicitly re-traverse** sections of the tape to look for previously processed areas — it has no random access.
- **Halting** is the only signal that the computation is complete; there is no implicit "answer ready" state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In a typical 14-mark question, the examiner expects you to (a) state the formal TM definition, (b) describe the high-level algorithm, (c) write the transition function for at least 3-4 key states, and (d) trace the TM on a small example. Skipping the trace is the #1 reason students lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops **exhaustive constructions** of Turing Machines for several canonical computable functions. Each derivation is written so a KTU student can reproduce it in an examination.

### 3.1 Example 1: Successor Function $f(n) = n + 1$ in Unary

**Problem:** Construct a TM that, given $0^n$ on the tape, replaces it with $0^{n+1}$.

**Tape Alphabet:** $\Gamma = \{0, 1, B\}$ (we use $1$ as a left-end marker).

**States:** $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_f\}$

**High-Level Algorithm:**
1. Move right to find the end of the input string.
2. Append a single $0$ at the rightmost position.
3. Move left to return to the start marker.
4. Halt in $q_f$.

**Transition Function $\delta$ (step-by-step derivation):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, 1) &= (q_0, 1, R) && \text{— scan past left marker} \\
\delta(q_0, 0) &= (q_1, 0, R) && \text{— first 0 found, enter scanning state} \\
\delta(q_1, 0) &= (q_1, 0, R) && \text{— keep scanning right} \\
\delta(q_1, B) &= (q_2, 0, L) && \text{— at blank, write a 0 (appending n+1) and go left} \\
\delta(q_2, 0) &= (q_2, 0, L) && \text{— rewind left past all 0s} \\
\delta(q_2, 1) &= (q_f, 1, R) && \text{— reached start marker, halt}
\end{aligned}$$

**Trace on input $n = 3$ (tape: `1 0 0 0 B B …`):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 0:} \quad & q_0 \; 1\,0\,0\,0\,B \\
\text{Step 1:} \quad & 1\,q_0\,0\,0\,0\,B \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & 1\,0\,q_1\,0\,0\,0\,B \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & 1\,0\,0\,q_1\,0\,0\,B \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & 1\,0\,0\,0\,q_1\,0\,B \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & 1\,0\,0\,0\,0\,q_2\,B \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & 1\,0\,0\,0\,q_2\,0\,0\,B \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & 1\,0\,0\,q_2\,0\,0\,0\,B \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & 1\,0\,q_2\,0\,0\,0\,0\,B \\
\text{Step 9:} \quad & 1\,q_2\,0\,0\,0\,0\,0\,B \\
\text{Step 10:} \quad & q_f\,1\,0\,0\,0\,0\,0\,B \quad \text{HALT}
\end{aligned}$$

The output tape contains `1 0 0 0 0 0`, which is $0^4$ = unary representation of 4 = $3 + 1$. ✓

### 3.2 Example 2: Addition $f(m, n) = m + n$ in Unary

**Input Encoding:** $0^m 1 0^n$ — the symbol $1$ separates the two operands.

**Algorithm:**
1. Scan right, locate the separator $1$.
2. Erase the separator.
3. Continue right to the end of the $0^n$ string.
4. Return to the start; the remaining tape is $0^{m+n}$.

**Transition Function $\delta$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, B) &= (q_0, B, R) && \text{— skip leading blanks} \\
\delta(q_0, 0) &= (q_1, 0, R) && \text{— find first 0, enter scan mode} \\
\delta(q_1, 0) &= (q_1, 0, R) && \text{— pass through m's zeros} \\
\delta(q_1, 1) &= (q_2, B, R) && \text{— erase the separator '1', go right} \\
\delta(q_2, 0) &= (q_2, 0, R) && \text{— pass through n's zeros} \\
\delta(q_2, B) &= (q_3, B, L) && \text{— hit blank, begin return} \\
\delta(q_3, 0) &= (q_3, 0, L) && \text{— rewind} \\
\delta(q_3, B) &= (q_f, B, R) && \text{— at leftmost blank, halt}
\end{aligned}$$

**Trace on input $m = 2, n = 3$ (tape: `B 0 0 1 0 0 0 B …`):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial:} \quad & B\,0\,0\,1\,0\,0\,0\,B \\
& B\,q_0\,0\,0\,1\,0\,0\,0\,B \\
& B\,0\,q_1\,0\,1\,0\,0\,0\,B \\
& B\,0\,0\,q_1\,1\,0\,0\,0\,B \\
& B\,0\,0\,1\,q_2\,0\,0\,0\,B \quad \text{(separator erased → B)} \\
& B\,0\,0\,B\,q_2\,0\,0\,0\,B \\
& B\,0\,0\,B\,0\,q_2\,0\,0\,B \\
& B\,0\,0\,B\,0\,0\,q_2\,0\,B \\
& B\,0\,0\,B\,0\,0\,0\,q_2\,B \\
& B\,0\,0\,B\,0\,0\,0\,B\,q_3 \quad \text{(begin return)} \\
& B\,q_3\,0\,0\,B\,0\,0\,0\,B \\
& q_f\,B\,0\,0\,B\,0\,0\,0\,B \quad \text{HALT — output } 0^5
\end{aligned}$$

The output is the contiguous non-blank string $0^5$ = unary 5 = $2 + 3$. ✓

### 3.3 Example 3: Proper Subtraction (Monus) $f(m, n) = m \dot{-} n$

This is more intricate because the TM must handle the case $m < n$ gracefully (output should be $0$, not a negative number).

**Input Encoding:** $0^m 1 0^n$.

**Algorithm (Repeated Erasure):**
1. Scan right; find the separator $1$.
2. Replace the separator with $B$ (blank).
3. Scan right; for each $0$ on the right side, scan left and erase **one** $0$ from the left.
4. If no $0$ remains on the right, the left side has $m - n$ zeros (or 0 if $m \le n$).
5. If the left side becomes empty **before** the right side is exhausted, output a single $0$.

**Skeleton Transition Function:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, 0) &= (q_1, 0, R) && \text{— find first 0} \\
\delta(q_1, 0) &= (q_1, 0, R) && \text{— skip m's zeros} \\
\delta(q_1, 1) &= (q_2, B, R) && \text{— erase separator} \\
\delta(q_2, 0) &= (q_3, B, R) && \text{— erase first 0 of n, mark} \\
\delta(q_3, 0) &= (q_3, 0, L) && \text{— return left to erase one 0 from m} \\
\delta(q_3, B) &= (q_4, B, L) && \text{— rewind} \\
\delta(q_4, 0) &= (q_5, B, R) && \text{— erase one 0 from m's section} \\
\delta(q_5, B) &= (q_2, B, R) && \text{— return to right section, repeat} \\
\delta(q_2, B) &= (q_6, B, L) && \text{— n is exhausted, output m-n zeros} \\
\delta(q_6, B) &= (q_f, B, R) && \text{— halt}
\end{aligned}$$

(For brevity, only the essential transitions are shown; a full design would handle edge cases like $n = 0$ and $m < n$ explicitly.)

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of TM Function Constructions

| Function | Input Encoding | Number of States | Key Technique | Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f(n) = n+1$ | $0^n$ | 4 | Scan right, append $0$ | Easy |
| $f(m, n) = m + n$ | $0^m 1 0^n$ | 5 | Erase separator | Easy |
| $f(m, n) = m \cdot n$ | $0^m 1 0^n$ | ~10 | Nested copy-erase loops | Medium |
| $f(m, n) = m \dot{-} n$ | $0^m 1 0^n$ | ~12 | Cross-cancel in pairs | Medium-Hard |
| $f(w) = ww$ | $w \in \Sigma^*$ | ~6 | Mark, return, copy | Medium |
| $f(n) = 2^n$ | $0^n$ | ~8 | Iterate successor, doubling | Hard |
| $f(n) = n!$ | $0^n$ | ~14 | Multiply decreasing sequence | Hard |
| $f(n) = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ | $0^n$ | ~10 | Repeated halving | Hard |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Shortcut:** When asked to "construct a TM" in KTU exams, you do **not** need to enumerate every transition. Present a **clear algorithm in 3-5 lines**, give the **transition function for 3-4 representative states**, and **trace on one small example**. This typically fetches 10-12 out of 14 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Architecture: TM as Function Computer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input_Phase [Input Phase]
        A[Tape Initialized with Input w] --> B[Read Write Head at Cell 1]
    end
    subgraph Compute_Phase [Computation Phase]
        B --> C{Apply Transition delta}
        C --> D[Update State q to q prime]
        C --> E[Write Symbol on Tape]
        C --> F[Move Head L or R]
        D --> G{Is q prime in F}
        E --> G
        F --> G
    end
    subgraph Output_Phase [Output Phase]
        G -->|Yes| H[Halt and Read Tape]
        G -->|No| C
        H --> I[Output equals f of w]
    end
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style I fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style G fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
```

### 4.2 State Transition Diagram — TM for Successor Function

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q0start
    q0start --> q0start : 1,1,R
    q0start --> q1scan : 0,0,R
    q1scan --> q1scan : 0,0,R
    q1scan --> q2append : B,0,L
    q2append --> q2append : 0,0,L
    q2append --> qfhalt : 1,1,R
    qfhalt --> [*] : HALT
```

**Legend (read each transition as):** `read_symbol, write_symbol, head_direction`

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — TM for $f(m, n) = m + n$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Tape_Contents [Tape Layout]
        T1[Cell 1: Leading Blank B]
        T2[Cell 2 to m plus 1: 0 repeated m times]
        T3[Cell m plus 2: Separator 1]
        T4[Cell m plus 3 to m plus n plus 2: 0 repeated n times]
        T5[Cell m plus n plus 3: Trailing Blank B]
        T1 --- T2 --- T3 --- T4 --- T5
    end
    subgraph TM_Logic [TM Transition Logic]
        S1[State q0: Skip Blanks] --> S2[State q1: Locate Separator 1]
        S2 --> S3[State q2: Erase 1 and Scan Rightmost Zeros]
        S3 --> S4[State q3: Return to Left Boundary]
        S4 --> S5[State qf: Halt and Emit 0 raised to m plus n]
    end
    Tape_Contents --> TM_Logic
```

### 4.4 Computational Flowchart — Monus $f(m, n) = m \dot{-} n$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Tape has 0 raised to m, 1, 0 raised to n] --> B{Find Separator 1}
    B -->|Found| C[Erase 1 to B]
    C --> D{Scan Right Side}
    D -->|Found 0| E[Erase One 0 from Right]
    E --> F[Return to Left Side]
    F --> G[Mark and Erase One 0 from Left]
    G --> D
    D -->|No 0 remaining on Right| H{Right side completely erased}
    H -->|Yes| I[Return Head to Start]
    I --> J[Halt in qf, Output equals 0 raised to max m minus n, 0]
    H -->|No| K[Output a single 0]
    K --> J
    style J fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style K fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
```

### 4.5 Class Hierarchy of TM-Computable Functions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[All Partial Functions over Sigma star] --> R[Partial Recursive Functions]
    P --> NR[Non Recursive Functions e.g. Halting Problem]
    R --> PR[Primitive Recursive Functions]
    R --> NPR[Non Primitive Recursive e.g. Ackermann]
    PR --> ADD[Addition]
    PR --> MUL[Multiplication]
    PR --> EXP[Exponentiation]
    PR --> FACT[Factorial bounded by input]
    NPR --> ACK[Ackermann Function]
    NPR --> UTM[Universal TM simulator]
    style R fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style PR fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style NR fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The set of **primitive recursive functions** corresponds to TMs that always halt (bounded loops), while **partial recursive functions** allow unbounded recursion (the $\mu$-operator, modeled by `while` loops or TM self-reference). The **Universal TM** is itself partial recursive but not primitive recursive.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a Turing-computable function. When is a function called total computable vs partial computable?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A function $f: \Sigma^* \to \Sigma^*$ is called **Turing-computable** if there exists a Turing Machine $M$ such that for every input $w$ in the domain of $f$, $M$ halts with $f(w)$ written on the tape.

- If $M$ halts on **every** $w \in \Sigma^*$, then $f$ is a **total computable function**.
- If $M$ halts only on a **subset** of $\Sigma^*$ (i.e., for some inputs, $M$ loops forever), then $f$ is a **partial computable function**.

The function value on inputs where $M$ does not halt is said to be **undefined**, denoted $f(w) = \uparrow$.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for total, 1 mark for partial distinction.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the blank symbol $B$ in a Turing Machine that computes numeric functions. Why is it indispensable?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The blank symbol $B$ plays three critical roles:

1. **Workspace Provision:** $B$ fills the unbounded tape beyond the input, giving the TM an infinite scratch area to write intermediate results — essential since a TM has no separate memory.
2. **Boundary Detection:** The first occurrence of $B$ tells the TM it has reached the end of the input or output region. This is used in functions like $f(n) = n+1$ to find where to append.
3. **Erasure:** Replacing a symbol with $B$ effectively "deletes" it. The monus function $m \dot{-} n$ uses $B$ to erase paired zeros from both sides.

Without $B$, the TM would have no way to identify the boundary of the input or to use the tape as unbounded memory.

*(Award 1 mark per role.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Construct a Turing Machine that computes the function $f(n) = n + 2$ in unary notation. Clearly state $Q$, $\Sigma$, $\Gamma$, the start state, the final state, and the transition function $\delta$. **Trace** the machine on input $n = 2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct a Turing Machine that computes the **copy function** $f(w) = ww$ for an input string $w \in \{0, 1\}^*$. Outline the high-level algorithm and provide the key transitions. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 marks:**

**Tape Alphabet:** $\Gamma = \{0, 1, B\}$ where $1$ is the left-end marker.

**States:** $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_f\}$

**Final State:** $F = \{q_f\}$

**Transition Function $\delta$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, 1) &= (q_0, 1, R) && \text{— pass left-end marker} \\
\delta(q_0, 0) &= (q_1, 0, R) && \text{— first 0 found, enter append mode} \\
\delta(q_1, 0) &= (q_1, 0, R) && \text{— continue scanning right} \\
\delta(q_1, B) &= (q_2, 0, L) && \text{— append first extra 0} \\
\delta(q_2, 0) &= (q_3, 0, R) && \text{— re-traverse to find rightmost blank} \\
\delta(q_3, 0) &= (q_3, 0, R) && \text{— continue right} \\
\delta(q_3, B) &= (q_4, 0, L) && \text{— append second extra 0} \\
\delta(q_4, 0) &= (q_4, 0, L) && \text{— rewind left} \\
\delta(q_4, 1) &= (q_f, 1, R) && \text{— reached marker, halt}
\end{aligned}$$

**Trace on $n = 2$ (input: `1 0 0 B`):**

| Step | Configuration | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $q_0\;1\,0\,0\,B$ | Start at left marker |
| 1 | $1\,q_0\,0\,0\,B$ | Pass marker |
| 2 | $1\,0\,q_1\,0\,B$ | First 0 found |
| 3 | $1\,0\,0\,q_1\,B$ | Scan right |
| 4 | $1\,0\,0\,0\,q_2$ | Write first extra 0, go left |
| 5 | $1\,0\,0\,q_2\,0\,0$ | Rewind |
| 6 | $1\,0\,q_2\,0\,0\,0$ | Continue rewinding |
| 7 | $1\,q_2\,0\,0\,0\,0$ | Rewinding |
| 8 | $q_f\,1\,0\,0\,0\,0$ | **HALT** with $0^4$ = $n+2$ ✓ |

*(Mark distribution: 1 for alphabet/states, 4 for transition function, 2 for trace.)*

---

**Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 marks:**

**High-Level Algorithm:**
1. Move right across $w$, marking each symbol in turn using markers $X$ (for 0) and $Y$ (for 1).
2. After marking, go to the right end of the tape (past all marked symbols and blanks).
3. Restore the original symbol at the marked position by writing back a copy of the original.
4. Continue until all of $w$ is copied to the right.

**Tape Layout Convention:**

| Left half (original) | Center marker | Right half (copy under construction) |
|---|---|---|
| $\ldots 0\,1\,0\,1 \ldots$ | $B$ | $\ldots 0\,1\,0\,1 \ldots$ |

**Key Transitions (abbreviated):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, a) &= (q_1, X_a, R) && \text{for } a \in \{0,1\}; X_0 = X, X_1 = Y \\
\delta(q_1, a) &= (q_1, a, R) && \text{— find end of original} \\
\delta(q_1, B) &= (q_2, a, L) && \text{— write a copy, then return} \\
\delta(q_2, a) &= (q_2, a, L) && \text{— return to marker} \\
\delta(q_2, X_a) &= (q_0, a, R) && \text{— restore original, repeat} \\
\delta(q_0, B) &= (q_f, B, R) && \text{— all copied, halt}
\end{aligned}$$

The full tape at halting is `w B w`, which represents the output $ww$. *(Mark distribution: 2 for algorithm, 3 for transitions, 2 for layout.)*

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **general architecture** of a Turing Machine that computes a function. Explain the role of the read/write head, the finite control, and the unbounded tape. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a Turing Machine for the function $f(m, n) = m \cdot n$ in unary. Outline the algorithm using **marker symbols** and explain the **nested-loop structure** on the tape. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Architecture (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):**

A TM computing a function has three components:

1. **Finite Control** — A finite state machine that holds the current state $q \in Q$. It directs the head movement and symbol writing.
2. **Tape** — A bi-infinite sequence of cells, each holding one symbol from $\Gamma$. It acts as both input medium, output medium, and **unlimited scratch space**.
3. **Read/Write Head** — A pointer to one cell at a time. It reads the symbol, writes a new one, and moves one cell left or right.

**Computation Cycle:** At each step, the head reads symbol $X$, the control looks up $\delta(q, X) = (q', Y, D)$, writes $Y$, transitions to $q'$, and moves in direction $D \in \{L, R\}$.

**Function Computation:** The TM starts with $w$ on the tape and $q_0$ as initial state. The **computation halts** when it reaches a configuration with $q \in F$. The non-blank portion of the tape at that moment is $f(w)$.

*(Mark distribution: 2 for diagram, 3 for component roles, 2 for computation cycle.)*

---

**Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Input:** $0^m\,1\,0^n$ on tape.

**Algorithm (nested loop):**
1. **Outer loop:** For each of the $m$ zeros in the left section, do:
2. **Inner loop:** Scan the right section ($0^n$) and append $n$ zeros to the output area on the right.
3. After all $m$ iterations, the output area contains $0^{m \cdot n}$.

**Implementation Sketch:**

- Use marker $A$ to mark the current $0$ of $m$ being processed.
- Use marker $B$ to mark the current $0$ of $n$ being copied.
- After copying all $n$ zeros (one cycle of inner loop), unmark all $B$'s and move left to find the next unmarked $0$ in $m$.

**Tape Layout During Computation:**

| Section 1 (input m) | Sep | Section 2 (input n) | Output (m times n) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0^{m-k}\,A\,0^k$ | $1$ | $0^{n-j}\,B\,0^j$ | $0^{k \cdot n + j}$ |

**Pseudo-Transitions:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_{\text{mark}}, 0) &= (q_{\text{mark}}, A, R) && \text{— mark next 0 of m} \\
\delta(q_{\text{copy}}, 0) &= (q_{\text{copy}}, B, R) && \text{— mark next 0 of n} \\
\delta(q_{\text{append}}, B) &= (q_{\text{append}}, 0, R) && \text{— append 0 to output} \\
\delta(q_{\text{reset}}, B) &= (q_{\text{mark}}, B, L) && \text{— inner loop done, next outer iter.}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** After halting, the tape contains `0^{m·n}` (after cleanup), demonstrating that multiplication is TM-computable. *(Mark distribution: 2 for algorithm, 2 for markers, 3 for transitions/layout.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the blank-handling transitions:** Many students define $\delta$ only on input symbols $\{0, 1\}$ and forget what happens on $B$. A complete TM must specify behavior for all $q \in Q$ and all symbols in $\Gamma$. Leaving transitions undefined often means the TM "crashes" silently.
> 2. **Confusing output with state:** The output is read from the **tape**, not from the **state**. The final state merely signals "computation finished"; the actual answer is the non-blank tape content.
> 3. **Skipping the trace:** Examiners allocate 2-3 marks for a clean trace. Even a 2-3 step trace on a small input shows you understand the dynamics.
> 4. **Mixing unary and binary notations:** Pick **one** representation and stick with it. Mixing `0101` (binary) with `0 0 0 0` (unary 4) in the same trace is a guaranteed mark deduction.
> 5. **Halting confusion:** A TM that runs off the right edge of the tape (if it keeps moving $R$ on $B$) does not "halt cleanly". Always design an explicit halting state and explicit transitions leading to it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **A TM computes a function** $f(w)$ if and only if it **halts** with $f(w)$ as the non-blank tape content.
- **TM-computable = (partial) recursive.** This equivalence is a foundational result, not a definition.
- **Turing-computable functions** include: $n+1$, $m+n$, $m \cdot n$, $m \dot{-} n$, $n!$, $2^n$, $ww$, $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$, Ackermann function, and all primitive recursive functions.
- **Encoding matters:** Numeric functions in TM examples are usually in **unary** ($0^n$ for $n$), since it is far easier to manipulate on a tape.
- **The blank $B$** is the TM's scratchpad — use it generously for separators and markers.
- **Marker symbols** ($X, Y, Z, A, B$ — capital letters) are conventional choices; they must be in $\Gamma$ but not in $\Sigma$.
- **Three halting conditions:** halt in final state (defined), infinite loop (undefined), crash (undefined).
- **Notation:** $M(w) = v$ or $f_M(w) = v$ means $M$ halts with $v$; $M(w) = \uparrow$ means $M$ does not halt.
- **Partial vs. Total:** A total computable function halts on every input; a partial computable function halts only on its domain.
- **The Halting Problem is undecidable** — there is no TM that, given $(M, w)$, always correctly decides whether $M$ halts on $w$.
- **Universal TM** — a single TM that can simulate any other TM given its description. This is the theoretical ancestor of the stored-program computer.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always include (i) formal 7-tuple, (ii) high-level algorithm, (iii) representative transitions, (iv) a small trace. This structure alone guarantees high marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variants of Turing Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT419 - INTRODUCTION TO THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Variants of Turing Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Standard Turing Machine (Recap)
A **Standard Turing Machine (TM)** is a 7-tuple mathematical abstraction defined as:

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$$

where:
- $Q$ is a finite, non-empty set of **states**.
- $\Sigma$ is a finite, non-empty set of **input symbols** (alphabet), not containing the blank symbol $B$.
- $\Gamma$ is a finite, non-empty set of **tape symbols**, where $\Sigma \subseteq \Gamma$.
- $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \longrightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ is the **transition function** (for deterministic TM).
- $q_0 \in Q$ is the **start state**.
- $B \in \Gamma - \Sigma$ is the **blank symbol**.
- $F \subseteq Q$ is the set of **final (accepting) states**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The *Church–Turing Thesis* asserts that any computational model that is "effectively calculable" can be simulated by a standard, single-tape, deterministic TM. **Variants of TMs** are alternative computational models that *extend* or *restrict* the standard TM. KTU's core expected learning outcome is to **prove that these variants do not add extra computational power** — they all recognize exactly the same class of languages: the **Recursively Enumerable (RE) languages**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **Standard Turing Machine** as a basic audio cassette player with a single read/write head and one tape strip. It is reliable, but limited. Now, think of **Variants of Turing Machines** as **upgraded versions** of this player:

- A **multitape TM** is like a multi-track recording studio — multiple tapes running in parallel.
- A **nondeterministic TM** is like a GPS that can compute *all possible routes at once* in some magical sense.
- A **semi-infinite tape TM** is a cassette player whose tape has a *fixed left end* (no infinite stretching to the left).
- A **multistack TM** is a TM equipped with a pile of LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) registers, much like a stack of plates.
- A **two-dimensional TM** is a player whose tape is an *infinite chessboard* that the head can move up, down, left, or right on.

The crucial insight — and the KTU exam-favorite point — is that **none of these upgrades make the machine more "powerful"** in terms of the languages it can recognize. They only differ in *speed* (and sometimes in the *clarity* of algorithm design).

> [!NOTE]
> **Computational Power vs. Computational Efficiency:** Adding a second tape does **not** increase the *class of languages recognized* (still only RE languages), but it can reduce the time complexity from $O(n^2)$ to $O(n \log n)$ in some cases. This is the famous **Linear Speedup Theorem** territory and is central to KTU Module 4 questions.

### 1.3 Why Study Variants?
- **Theoretical Insight:** Variants confirm the robustness of the Church–Turing Thesis.
- **Pedagogical Utility:** Some algorithms (e.g., palindrome checking, copying strings) are *much simpler* to write on a multitape TM.
- **Foundation for Complexity Theory:** Variants like nondeterminism give rise to the famous **P vs. NP** problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus Areas:** (1) Multitape TM, (2) Nondeterministic TM, (3) Semi-infinite tape TM, (4) Multistack TM. Students are expected to understand the **simulation construction** (how one TM simulates another) and the **complexity** of the simulation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of TM Variants

The variants covered under KTU's Module 4 can be classified based on the **dimension of extension** they apply to the standard TM model:

| # | Variant | Modified Component | KTU Relevance |
|---|---------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Multitape TM | Number of tapes & heads (k tapes) | Very High |
| 2 | Nondeterministic TM (NTM) | Transition function $\delta$ becomes a relation | Very High |
| 3 | Semi-infinite TM | Tape is bounded on one side | High |
| 4 | Multistack TM | Replaces tape with k LIFO stacks | High |
| 5 | Multi-head TM | Multiple heads on a single tape | Medium |
| 6 | Two-dimensional TM | Tape is a 2D grid | Low |

### 2.2 Formal Specification of Each Variant

#### 2.2.1 Multitape Turing Machine
A **k-tape TM** is defined as:

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$$

where the transition function is now:

$$\delta : Q \times \Gamma^k \longrightarrow Q \times \Gamma^k \times \{L, R\}^k$$

At each step, $M$ reads **one symbol from each of the $k$ tapes**, writes one symbol on each, and moves each head independently in $\{L, R\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> The input is normally placed on tape 1; all other tapes start entirely blank.

#### 2.2.2 Nondeterministic Turing Machine (NTM)
The transition function is replaced by a *relation*:

$$\delta : Q \times \Gamma \longrightarrow 2^{Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}}$$

At each configuration, the machine may *branch* into multiple possible next configurations. The NTM **accepts** an input $w$ if **at least one** computation branch leads to an accepting state.

#### 2.2.3 Semi-infinite Tape TM
The tape is bounded at the **left** end. The head starts at the leftmost cell, which is also the start position. Transitions that would move left from cell 0 leave the head at cell 0.

#### 2.2.4 Multistack TM
Has $k$ LIFO **stacks** instead of tapes. A stack is a tape where the head can only read the top symbol, write a new top, and move the head one cell up (push) or down (pop). A **two-stack TM** is famously equivalent to a standard TM.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Theorem / Property | Formal Statement | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|
| **Multitape TM ≡ Standard TM** | A $k$-tape TM with time complexity $T(n)$ can be simulated by a single-tape TM in $O(T(n)^2)$ | Very High |
| **NTM ≡ DTM** | A nondeterministic TM with time $T(n)$ can be simulated by a deterministic TM in $2^{O(T(n))}$ time | Very High |
| **Semi-infinite TM ≡ Standard TM** | A standard TM with time $T(n)$ can be simulated by a semi-infinite TM in $2T(n) + n$ time | High |
| **Multistack TM Equivalence** | A TM with $k$ stacks simulates a TM with $k$ tapes. Conversely, a TM with $k$ tapes is simulated by a TM with $2k$ stacks | High |
| **Two-stack TM = Standard TM** | A two-stack TM can simulate any standard TM | High |
| **Church–Turing Thesis** | All effectively computable functions are TM-computable | Foundational |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note on Table Notation:** When a transition involves *reading from all $k$ tapes simultaneously*, the formal symbol is $(q_1, a_1, a_2, \dots, a_k) \rightarrow (q_2, b_1, b_2, \dots, b_k, D_1, D_2, \dots, D_k)$, where each $D_i \in \{L, R\}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility
- **Multitape TM** → inspiration for the **Random Access Machine (RAM)** model used in modern algorithm analysis.
- **NTM** → directly motivates the complexity class **NP**.
- **Multistack TM** → foundation for **pushdown automata** and the design of **compiler parsers**.
- **Two-stack TM** → direct connection to the design of **memory hierarchies** in modern CPUs (call stack + operand stack).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Theorem: A Multitape TM Can Be Simulated by a Standard TM

**Statement.** Let $M$ be a $k$-tape TM with time complexity $T(n)$. There exists a standard single-tape TM $S$ that simulates $M$ with time complexity $O(T(n)^2)$.

#### 3.1.1 Construction Overview
The standard TM $S$ uses a single tape divided into $k$ **virtual tracks** by introducing a new marker symbol (e.g., $\#$) to separate the tracks. A multi-head marker (e.g., $X$) is placed above the currently scanned cell of each virtual tape.

#### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Encoding the tape of $M$ on the tape of $S$.**

The tape of $S$ holds the contents of all $k$ tapes of $M$, arranged as $k$ tracks. The layout for $k=2$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Track 1 (Virtual Tape 1):} &\quad \# \, a_1 \, a_2 \, a_3 \, \dots \, a_n \, B \, B \, B \dots \\
\text{Track 2 (Virtual Tape 2):} &\quad \# \, b_1 \, b_2 \, b_3 \, \dots \, b_n \, B \, B \, B \dots
\end{aligned}
$$

Each cell of $S$ thus stores a pair $(a_i, b_i)$ using a new alphabet $\Gamma' = \Gamma \cup \Gamma^2 \cup \{\#\}$, where $\#$ is the track separator.

**Step 2 — Storing the head positions of $M$.**

For each virtual tape $i$, mark the cell currently under head $i$ of $M$ by a dotted symbol (e.g., a "dot" above the symbol). Conceptually:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{a}_j \equiv \text{``head 1 of } M \text{ is at cell } j \text{ of tape 1''}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Reading symbols under all $k$ heads of $M$.**

To simulate one move of $M$, $S$ must:
1. Sweep the entire tape from the leftmost marked cell to the rightmost marked cell.
2. At each marked cell, record the virtual symbol underneath.
3. After one full sweep, $S$ knows all $k$ symbols currently read by $M$.

This step requires traversing the entire **used** portion of the tape, which can be of length up to $T(n)$.

**Step 4 — Making the transition.**

Using the recorded $k$-tuple of symbols, $S$ looks up the corresponding transition of $M$ in its finite control. Since $M$ is deterministic in this construction, the next state, the new symbols, and the directions are uniquely determined.

**Step 5 — Updating the tape and head markers.**

$S$ makes a *second* full sweep from left to right, this time:
- Replacing each marked cell's symbol with the new symbol dictated by $\delta_M$.
- Moving the dot one cell left or right according to the direction given by $\delta_M$.

**Step 6 — Bounding the time complexity.**

- Each of the two sweeps in Steps 3 and 5 traverses at most $T(n)$ cells (since $M$ cannot have moved a head further than $T(n)$ cells in $T(n)$ steps).
- Therefore, simulating **one step of $M$** takes $O(T(n))$ time for $S$.
- To simulate $T(n)$ steps of $M$, $S$ takes $O(T(n)) \times T(n) = O(T(n)^2)$ time.

$$
\boxed{\text{Time}(S) = O\bigl(T(n)^2\bigr)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Pearl:** The $O(T(n)^2)$ bound is *tight* in the worst case (palindromes, for instance) but is also the *best known deterministic simulation* in the general case. This is the basis of the famous **time-hierarchy** separation results.

### 3.2 Theorem: A Nondeterministic TM Can Be Simulated by a Deterministic TM

**Statement.** If a nondeterministic TM $N$ decides language $L$ in time $T(n)$, then there exists a deterministic TM $D$ that decides $L$ in time $2^{O(T(n))}$.

#### 3.2.1 Construction (Bounded by Tape Address)

Each computation branch of $N$ on input $w$ of length $n$ can be represented as a **sequence of choices**. Since $N$ is nondeterministic, suppose it has at most $c$ choices at each step (for some constant $c$ determined by $\Gamma$ and $Q$). A computation of length at most $T(n)$ can be encoded as a string over an alphabet of size $c$, of length at most $T(n)$.

**Step 1 — Enumerate all candidate computation sequences.**

The deterministic TM $D$ generates, in lexicographic order, all strings of length $1, 2, 3, \dots, T(n)$ over the alphabet $\{1, 2, \dots, c\}$.

**Step 2 — Test each candidate.**

For each candidate string $s$ of length $k$:
1. Reset the simulated configuration of $N$ to the initial configuration on $w$.
2. For $i = 1$ to $k$, follow the $s[i]$-th choice in the transition of $N$ at step $i$.
3. If at any point the branch rejects (no valid move), abandon $s$.
4. If at any point the branch reaches an accepting state, **$D$ accepts**.

**Step 3 — Bound the running time.**

- The number of candidate sequences of length $\leq T(n)$ is at most $c^{T(n)} = 2^{O(T(n))}$.
- Each candidate takes $O(T(n))$ time to verify.
- Total time:

$$
\text{Time}(D) = O\bigl(c^{T(n)} \cdot T(n)\bigr) = 2^{O(T(n))}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This *exponential blow-up* is fundamental. It is the technical reason why the **P vs. NP** question is open — we do not know if a polynomial-time deterministic simulation of NTMs is possible.

### 3.3 Theorem: A Semi-infinite Tape TM is Equivalent to a Standard TM

**Statement.** A semi-infinite tape TM (left-bounded tape) can simulate a standard TM with at most a constant-factor slowdown, and vice versa.

#### 3.3.1 Construction of Standard TM from Semi-infinite TM (the harder direction)

Let $M$ be a standard TM with two-way infinite tape. We construct a semi-infinite TM $S$.

**Step 1 — Folding the tape.**

Imagine folding the standard TM's two-way infinite tape at the starting position, creating a left half (cells $-1, -2, \dots$) and a right half (cells $0, 1, 2, \dots$). The right half stays as is; the left half is *reflected* and stored to the right of the right half.

The new layout on $S$'s semi-infinite tape:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Position: } 0 \;\; 1 \;\; 2 \;\; 3 \;\; \dots \;\; n-1 \;\; n \;\; n+1 \;\; n+2 \dots \\
\text{Content: } a_0 \; a_1 \; a_2 \; a_3 \dots a_{n-1} \; a_{-1} \; a_{-2} \; a_{-3} \dots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Marking the fold.**

A special marker $\#$ is placed at position $n$ (the boundary between cell $-1$ and cell $0$ of $M$).
