```mermaid
    Q4 -- Yes, always halts --> RE["CLASS: RECURSIVE"]
    Q4 -- Yes, may not halt --> REN["CLASS: TYPE 0 RE"]
    Q4 -- No --> NR["CLASS: NON RE"]
    R --> END["FINAL CLASSIFICATION"]
    CF --> END
    CS --> END
    RE --> END
    REN --> END
    NR --> END
```

## 4.4 Membership Test Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Input["INPUT STRING w, LANGUAGE L"] --> Machine["Run the appropriate recognizer"]
    Machine --> Halts{"Does it halt?"}
    Halts -- Yes, Accept --> InL["w is in L"]
    Halts -- Yes, Reject --> OutL["w is NOT in L DECIDED"]
    Halts -- No loops --> Uncertain["MEMBERSHIP UNDECIDED FOR RE"]
    InL --> Done["RESULT"]
    OutL --> Done
    Uncertain --> Done
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The arrows in the hierarchy diagrams denote the **set-theoretic subset relation** — every language in a lower class is also in all higher classes. The boundary lines emphasize **strict containment** (proper subset).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define a Recursive Language. Give one example of a recursive language that is not regular.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> A language $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$ is called **Recursive** if there exists a Turing Machine $M$ that halts on every input string $w \in \Sigma^*$ and accepts $w$ if $w \in L$, rejects $w$ if $w \notin L$.
>
> **Example:** $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$.
> - This language is not regular (fails the Pumping Lemma).
> - It is recursive because we can construct a TM that scans left-to-right, matches each $a$ with a $b$ using its tape, and halts (accepting or rejecting) on every input.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is the difference between a Recursive language and a Recursively Enumerable language?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Recursive | Recursively Enumerable |
> |--------|-----------|------------------------|
> | Halting | TM halts on **every** input | TM halts only on strings in $L$ |
> | Complement | Closed under complement | **Not** closed under complement |
> | Decider | Yes (Total TM) | Only a recognizer (may not halt on rejection) |
>
> Every recursive language is RE, but there exist RE languages (e.g., HALT) that are not recursive.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Chomsky Hierarchy of formal languages. For each type, state the grammar form, the corresponding language class, and the recognizing machine.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Prove that the set of Recursively Enumerable Languages is **not** closed under complement.

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — The Chomsky Hierarchy [7 marks]

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1: Introduction [1 mark]**
> The Chomsky Hierarchy, proposed by Noam Chomsky in 1956, classifies formal grammars into four types (Type 0, 1, 2, 3) based on restrictions on production rules. Each type corresponds to a language class and a recognizing automaton.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2: Type 0 — Unrestricted Grammar [1.5 marks]**
> - **Production Form:** $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ where $\alpha, \beta \in (V \cup T)^*$ and $\vert \alpha \vert \geq 1$.
> - **Language Class:** Recursively Enumerable (RE).
> - **Machine:** Turing Machine (TM).
> - **Example Language:** HALT problem, $\{a^{2^n} \mid n \geq 0\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3: Type 1 — Context-Sensitive Grammar [1.5 marks]**
> - **Production Form:** $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ where $\gamma \neq \epsilon$ and $\vert \alpha A \beta \vert \leq \vert \alpha \gamma \beta \vert$ (monotonic / non-contracting).
> - **Language Class:** Context-Sensitive Language (CSL).
> - **Machine:** Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA).
> - **Example Language:** $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 4: Type 2 — Context-Free Grammar [1.5 marks]**
> - **Production Form:** $A \rightarrow \gamma$ where $A \in V$ and $\gamma \in (V \cup T)^*$.
> - **Language Class:** Context-Free Language (CFL).
> - **Machine:** Pushdown Automaton (PDA).
> - **Example Language:** $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 5: Type 3 — Regular Grammar [1 mark]**
> - **Production Form:** Right-linear: $A \rightarrow aB \mid a$, or left-linear: $A \rightarrow Ba \mid a$.
> - **Language Class:** Regular Language.
> - **Machine:** Finite Automaton (DFA/NFA).
> - **Example Language:** $(a+b)^*$, strings ending in $a$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 6: Containment Summary [0.5 mark]**
> $$\text{Regular} \subsetneq \text{CFL} \subsetneq \text{CSL} \subsetneq \text{RE}$$

#### Part (b) — RE is Not Closed Under Complement [7 marks]

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem:** There exists a recursively enumerable language whose complement is not recursively enumerable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Proof:**
> We prove this by contradiction using the **Halting Problem**.
>
> **Define:**
> $$L_{HALT} = \{ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ halts on input } w \}$$
>
> **Claim 1: $L_{HALT}$ is RE. [1.5 marks]**
> Construct TM $H$ that, on input $\langle M, w \rangle$, simulates $M$ on $w$. If $M$ halts, $H$ accepts. If $M$ loops, $H$ loops. Hence $H$ recognizes $L_{HALT}$, so $L_{HALT} \in \text{RE}$.
>
> **Claim 2: Assume, for contradiction, that $\overline{L_{HALT}}$ is also RE. [1 mark]**
> Then there exists a TM $\overline{H}$ that recognizes $\overline{L_{HALT}}$.
>
> **Construct diagonal machine $D$: [2 marks]**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> D(\langle M \rangle): & \\
> & \text{1. Run } \overline{H} \text{ on } \langle M, \langle M \rangle \rangle. \\
> & \text{2. If } \overline{H} \text{ accepts, } D \text{ rejects.} \\
> & \text{3. If } \overline{H} \text{ rejects or loops, } D \text{ accepts.}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Apply $D$ to its own description $\langle D \rangle$: [1.5 marks]**
> - If $D(\langle D \rangle)$ halts and accepts $\Rightarrow$ $\overline{H}$ accepted $\langle D, \langle D \rangle \rangle$ $\Rightarrow$ $D$ does not halt on $\langle D \rangle$. **Contradiction.**
> - If $D(\langle D \rangle)$ halts and rejects $\Rightarrow$ $\overline{H}$ rejected $\langle D, \langle D \rangle \rangle$ $\Rightarrow$ $D$ halts and accepts $\langle D \rangle$. **Contradiction.**
> - If $D(\langle D \rangle)$ loops $\Rightarrow$ $\overline{H}$ also loops on $\langle D, \langle D \rangle \rangle$ $\Rightarrow$ $D$ should accept (by definition). **Contradiction.**
>
> **Conclusion: [1 mark]**
> All cases lead to contradiction. Therefore, $\overline{L_{HALT}} \notin \text{RE}$, and the class RE is **not closed under complement**. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define Recursive and Recursively Enumerable Languages. Prove that every recursive language is recursively enumerable, but the converse is not true.

**(b) [7 Marks]** State the closure properties of (i) Recursive Languages, and (ii) Recursively Enumerable Languages, with brief justification.

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Definitions and Strict Inclusion [7 marks]

> [!NOTE]
> **Recursive Language [1 mark]:** $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$ is recursive if there exists a Turing Machine $M$ that halts on every input $w$ and decides whether $w \in L$ (accept) or $w \notin L$ (reject).

> [!NOTE]
> **Recursively Enumerable Language [1 mark]:** $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$ is RE if there exists a TM $M$ that halts and accepts every $w \in L$, and either rejects or loops for $w \notin L$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Proof 1: $\text{REC} \subseteq \text{RE}$ [2 marks]**
> Let $L \in \text{REC}$. Then there exists a decider TM $M$ that halts on all inputs.
> Define a recognizer $M'$ for $L$ as follows: $M'$ simulates $M$ on input $w$. Since $M$ always halts:
> - If $M$ accepts, $M'$ accepts.
> - If $M$ rejects, $M'$ rejects.
> Thus $M'$ recognizes $L$, so $L \in \text{RE}$. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Proof 2: $\text{REC} \neq \text{RE}$ [3 marks]**
> We exhibit an RE language that is not recursive. Consider the **Halting Problem:**
> $$L_{HALT} = \{ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ halts on } w \}$$
>
> - $L_{HALT} \in \text{RE}$ (as shown by the universal TM simulator).
> - $L_{HALT} \notin \text{REC}$: If it were recursive, $\overline{L_{HALT}}$ would also be recursive (REC is closed under complement), making both RE. But $\overline{L_{HALT}} \notin \text{RE}$ (proved by diagonalization), contradiction.
>
> Hence $L_{HALT} \in \text{RE} \setminus \text{REC}$, giving $\text{REC} \subsetneq \text{RE}$. $\blacksquare$

#### Part (b) — Closure Properties [7 marks]

> [!NOTE]
> **(i) Closure Properties of Recursive Languages [3.5 marks]**
>
> | Operation | Closed? | Justification |
> |-----------|---------|---------------|
> | Union | **Yes** | Run deciders for $L_1, L_2$ sequentially; accept if either accepts. |
> | Intersection | **Yes** | Run sequentially; accept if both accept. |
> | Complement | **Yes** | Swap accept/reject states of the decider. |
> | Concatenation | **Yes** | Nondeterministically split $w = uv$, run $L_1$-decider on $u$, $L_2$-decider on $v$. |
> | Kleene Star | **Yes** | Nondeterministically split $w = w_1 \dots w_k$; run decider on each $w_i$. |
> | Reversal | **Yes** | Reverse input then run decider. |

> [!NOTE]
> **(ii) Closure Properties of Recursively Enumerable Languages [3.5 marks]**
>
> | Operation | Closed? | Justification |
> |-----------|---------|---------------|
> | Union | **Yes** | Nondeterministically choose branch; run $M_1$ or $M_2$. |
> | Intersection | **Yes** | Run both recognizers in parallel; accept if both halt-accept. |
> | **Complement** | **No** | If closed, every RE language would be recursive (since $L$ and $\overline{L}$ both RE $\Rightarrow$ both recursive). But HALT is RE, not recursive. |
> | Concatenation | **Yes** | Nondeterministically split, run recognizers. |
> | Kleene Star | **Yes** | Nondeterministically decompose, run recognizer. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often claim RE is closed under complement — this is the **most frequently lost mark** in 14-mark questions. Always show the diagonalization argument.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing the **closure of REC under complement** with the **non-closure of RE under complement**. Memorize: "**RE is not closed under complement, but REC is.**"
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Failing to explicitly state that every CSL is recursive, and every recursive language is RE. This is essential to justify the hierarchy.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** In the Chomsky Hierarchy, students sometimes reverse the order or misattribute the machine (e.g., PDA for regular languages). Always remember: **DFA→Regular, PDA→CFL, LBA→CSL, TM→RE**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Recursive Language (REC):** Decidable by a Total Turing Machine that **always halts**. Closed under all standard operations including complement.

- **Recursively Enumerable Language (RE):** Recognized by a TM that halts on every $w \in L$ but **may loop on $w \notin L$**. **NOT closed under complement.**

- **Strict Inclusion:** $\text{REC} \subsetneq \text{RE} \subsetneq \mathcal{P}(\Sigma^*)$.

- **HALT Language:** $L_{HALT} \in \text{RE} \setminus \text{REC}$ — the canonical example of an RE-but-not-recursive language.

- **Diagonalization Argument:** Used to prove that $\overline{L_{HALT}} \notin \text{RE}$, establishing that RE is not closed under complement.

- **Chomsky Hierarchy (ascending order of power):**
  $$\text{Regular} \subsetneq \text{CFL} \subsetneq \text{CSL} \subsetneq \text{RE}$$

- **Machines ↔ Languages:**
  - **DFA / NFA** $\longleftrightarrow$ **Regular Languages** (Type 3)
  - **PDA** $\longleftrightarrow$ **Context-Free Languages** (Type 2)
  - **LBA** $\longleftrightarrow$ **Context-Sensitive Languages** (Type 1)
  - **TM (Decider)** $\longleftrightarrow$ **Recursive Languages**
  - **TM (Recognizer)** $\longleftrightarrow$ **Recursively Enumerable Languages** (Type 0)

- **Production Rule Constraints (memory aid — "Non-Contracting Rises"):**
  - **Type 0:** No restriction.
  - **Type 1:** Length must be non-decreasing ($\vert \alpha \beta \vert \leq \vert \alpha' \beta' \vert$).
  - **Type 2:** Left side must be a single non-terminal.
  - **Type 3:** Right side has at most one non-terminal at an end (linear).

- **Examples for Quick Recall:**
  - Regular: $(a+b)^*$, strings with even number of $a$'s.
  - CFL: $\{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$, balanced parentheses.
  - CSL: $\{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$, $a^{n!}$.
  - RE (non-recursive): HALT, $L_u = \{ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ accepts } w \}$.

- **Membership Test Outcome:**
  - Type 0 (RE): **Undecidable** in general.
  - Type 1 (CSL): **Decidable** (PSPACE).
  - Type 2 (CFL): **Decidable** via CYK in $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$.
  - Type 3 (Regular): **Decidable** in $\mathcal{O}(n)$.

- **Exam Strategy Tip:** When asked to "prove" a closure failure, always start with a **well-known undecidable problem** (HALT), then apply the assumed closure to derive a contradiction via diagonalization.
